channel.title: Zeteo
channel.description: Mehdi Hasan's Zeteo — First Draft and news analysis

item.title: Trump Is Dangerously Unwell. So Why Are These Dems Helping Him?
item.description: On this day in 2020, a federal judge in Portland barred President Donald Trump's federal agents from targeting journalists and legal observers, amid his crackdown on a U.S. city. Does this… sound familiar? Happy Thursday, everyone. It’s Swin and Andrew here. It’s obvious our current state of…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2020, a federal judge in Portland barred President Donald Trump's federal agents from targeting journalists and legal observers, amid his crackdown on a U.S. city. Does this… sound familiar?

Happy Thursday, everyone. It’s Swin and Andrew here. It’s obvious our current state of affairs is unsustainable.

The entire ruling party is propping up the personality cult and blundering agenda of someone who is disturbed to the point he’s willing to blow up the world economy for no reason beyond his own spite and confusion. Democrats, for their part, are not only struggling to oppose Donald Trump or rein him in… some are outright doing his bidding.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we once again acknowledge the reality that the president is deeply unwell and fundamentally unfit for office, while some Democrats insist on helping him. Meanwhile, the president unveiled his plan for spiking drug prices, and Jeffrey Epstein’s model recruiter was just found dead.

We’ve written here at Zeteo about how the sitting leader of the United States is “too dangerous, too unwell, too much of a fascist” and “ must be removed from office.” No one with any power is trying to remove Donald Trump from office, but that does not make it any less necessary: He is patently insane.

The president of the United States is trying to unilaterally impose new taxes on the American people because he’s mad at Canada for being on fire. He is angrily threatening war crimes and re-escalating his war in Iran at the worst possible time for the global economy – even though he knows the war is politically destroying him, his party, and his administration.

He is now ranting on social media about how voters should be grateful that his disastrous war has only gotten 18 Americans killed. He’s still suggesting he wants to run for an unconstitutional third term, and claiming now that “communists” want to “blow up” Mount Rushmore.

Every day Trump is in power is a unique, extreme crisis, one that threatens the lives and safety of an incalculable number of people, both here and abroad. And yet, some Democrats keep trying to give this man more power and more money, and expand his ability to threaten our democracy.

Yesterday, six House Democrats voted to give Trump another $1.15 trillion for defense, including $73 billion meant to help cover the rising costs of his failing, economy-destroying war. This same defense policy bill would further integrate the U.S. and Israeli militaries.

Six conservative Democrats voted for the legislation: Henry Cuellar of Texas; Don Davis of North Carolina; Marie Gluesenkamp Perez of Washington; Jared Golden of Maine, who’s retiring; Vicente Gonzalez of Texas; and Adam Gray of California. Without their votes, the defense policy bill would have failed.

And even more Democrats voted in favor of Trump’s voter suppression agenda – helping pass Republicans’ Voter ID Act, which was stuffed into a bill that otherwise purports to limit insider trading by members of Congress.

The legislation would require voters to show photo ID before casting a ballot in a federal election. That may sound fine, but as the Campaign Legal Center explains, “the list of acceptable forms of identification in the Voter ID Act is unreasonably narrow – and significantly more restrictive than almost every state voter ID law currently in place.”

“It excludes widely used forms of identification such as student, employee, and public assistance IDs, even though young people and voters of color disproportionately rely on these IDs and many states with voter ID requirements accept them,” the watchdog group writes. It adds that while the bill supposedly allows for Native Americans to use tribal IDs to vote, many tribal IDs wouldn’t qualify, because they don’t have a photo or expiration date.

Keep in mind: This measure solves no need whatsoever, and is purely designed to help Trump and Republicans keep people from voting.

Thirteen Democrats voted for this bill anyway, including five named above: Cuellar, Davis, Golden, Gonzalez, and Gluesenkamp Perez. The other eight are Kathy Castor of Florida; Josh Gottheimer of New Jersey; Marcy Kaptur of Ohio; Susie Lee of Nevada; Jared Moskowitz of Florida; Chris Pappas of New Hampshire; Darren Soto of Florida; and Derek Tran of California.

The voter ID measure is likely to stall in the Senate, where Democrats can filibuster. Congress is about to get started on its August recess.

We work at Zeteo, which means we won’t get reprimanded by our bosses for writing or saying certain things that are obviously, observably true, even if it might sound “biased” to people who think words are illegal if Donald Trump says they are. We are grateful for that privilege.

It’s why we can admit that Donald J. Trump is too mentally unwell and unstable, too much of an authoritarian nutcase, too much of a cartoonishly corrupt doofus, to be our president.

In traditional, mainstream media, there isn’t really an official, respectable vocabulary with which to communicate the severity of this crisis. And maybe that’s the problem, and part of why and how we got here, and part of how and why some Democrats feel comfortable voting with this moronic fascist.

  • Endless money for war:The Republican-led Housepasseda $1.15 trillion defense policy bill with $73 billion expected to help cover the costs of Trump’s war with Iran.
  • Houthis attack:Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthissaidthey attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea – the first strikes of their kind since the Houthis announced ablockade on Saudi ships. The attacks sent oil prices surging.
  • Strait talk:Trumpthreatenedto bomb one Iranian bridge or power plant “any time” Iran attacks a ship in the Strait of Hormuz. Secretary of State Marco Rubiosaidtoday, “The price on Iran will get higher every night until they come to their senses.”
  • Threat returned:Iransaidit would respond by hitting bridges and energy infrastructure in the region.
  • Mind reader:Speaking about the 18 U.S. soldiers killed since he launched this war, Trumpsaid, “All of them said very strongly, ‘We cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon.’”
  • Tariff mania:A day after hitting Canada, Trumpannouncedimported generic drugs will face a 100% tariff starting in 2028, unless drugmakers move production to the U.S. Meanwhile, his trade chief, Jamieson Greer,refusedto concede that tariffs have raised costs on Americans.
  • Lawfare:House Judiciary Chairman Jim JordanurgedTrump's DOJ to prosecute Jack Smith, who led two criminal cases against Trump. “This referral, if ever brought before a judge, will get laughed out of court,” Rep. Jamie Raskinresponded.
  • Targeting the left:The GOP-led House Ways and Means Committeesenta subpoena to the left-wing news site Breakthrough News. The sitesaidthe committee is “demanding internal communications, financial records, and more,” calling it a “direct attack” on the First Amendment.
  • Maine latest:Troy Jackson, Maine Democrats’ new presumptive Senate nominee, reportedraising$1 million since launching his bid against Susan Collins,more thanhe raised in his unsuccessful bid for governor. In an interview on Capitol Hill, CollinscalledJackson a “Bernie Bro.”
  • Khan to city hall:Zohran MamdaniappointedLina Khan, the antitrust champion and youngest-ever FTC chair, to lead the board of New York City’s Economic Development Corporation, an agency long criticized as being too cozy with business. Khan will help steer projects, including the city’s five planned city-run grocery stores.
  • Epstein recruiter dead:Daniel Siad, the French model recruiter who introduced young women to Jeffrey Epstein, and whose name appears over 2,000 times in the DOJ’s Epstein files, wasfound deadnear Paris.

What is the only state in the U.S. that hasn’t voted for a Republican presidential candidate in the last 50 years?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Despite objections from Israeli environmental officials, construction of Itamar Ben-Gvir’s crocodile-infested moat isunderwayoutside of Ketziot prison’s high-security wing. When Ben-Gvir firstproposedthe moat, he specifically cited Florida’s Alligator Alcatraz, which has since beenshut down.
  • 🇵🇸‘Unacceptable provocation’:Ben-Gvir todayvisitedthe Al-Aqsa Mosque compound, praising hundreds of Jewish worshippers who had prayed at the site, despite strict rules barring non-Muslims from praying there. Jordan condemned the visit as “a desecration of the sanctity of the mosque, an ‌escalation, a barbaric act, and unacceptable provocation.”
  • 🇻🇪 May it please the court:The federal judge for Nicolás Maduro’s case officially set thetrial datefor June 2027. Maduro’s lawyers are expected to argue that he has immunity as a head of state – a debate that could find itself in the Supreme Court.

  • 🇲🇲

    Vanished at sea:More than500 Rohingya migrantsare now feared to be dead, after people from the persecuted Muslim minority group boarded two boats off the coast of Myanmar, attempting to escape the country’s ongoing violence. The UN says the boats sank in early July. As Myanmar researcher Lynn Htetsaidto theNYT,“If the people on the boats were from the West, many countries would have come together to form search groups. But Rohingya … are invisible people.”

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:Over 1,000 people have nowdiedin the Ebola outbreak in Congo. As of Monday, 2,473 cases have been recorded, and at least 737 patients have been hospitalized.

How bad is Trump doing? Well, a Fox (!) poll found voters would trust Democrats to do a better job than Republicans on most issues… including immigration.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald and Lara Strydom contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Bari Weiss’s Latest Hire Has an N-Word Problem
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we look at the controversial past of Bari Weiss’s latest hire – and what it says about the type of…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we look at the controversial past of Bari Weiss’s latest hire – and what it says about the type of staff she’s looking to build at CBS News. Additionally, why is the White House Correspondents’ Association hosting the greatest threat to press freedom in America? Also, CNNers are feeling cautiously optimistic following the latest WarnerMount merger developments.

Shortly after CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss announced that she had hired Mike Pesca – the host of the long-running daily news podcast ‘The Gist’ – to be the network’s editorial director of podcasts, one of Pesca’s former colleagues chimed in with a biting observation.

“‘What experience qualifies you for this job?’ ‘Well at Slate I died on the hill of being able to say n—,’” The Ringer’s Joel Anderson tweeted on Friday. “‘Say no more. Your offer is on the way.’”

Indeed, Pesca and ‘The Gist’ were suspended indefinitely by Slate in February 2021 after he argued with his fellow colleagues that white people should be allowed to say the n-word in certain situations. He would ultimately part ways with Slate in September of that year while also purchasing the podcast – which he would continue to host until he ditched it to work for Weiss.

“I’m not surprised,” one CBS News insider told Zeteo about Weiss’s embrace of Pesca to lead the network’s budding podcast division.

Perhaps more than anything, though, Pesca’s hiring lays bare that Weiss – the “radical centrist” founder of the anti-woke, pro-Israel blog The Free Press – is going out of her way to bring on a very specific kind of candidate: hires who were once part of the liberal establishment but grew skeptical of the “woke” orthodoxy foisted upon them, only to inevitably see themselves “canceled” by the left-wing “mob” at one point over their opinions on hot-button cultural topics.

It also generally helps if new employees share their self-avowed “Zionist fanatic” boss’s worldview on Israel and Gaza. Pesca, for his part, has pushed back on criticism that he’s gone “too easy” on Israel. At the same time, he’s justified not describing Gaza as a genocide by noting other wars have had greater death tolls, all while saying this is “not to deny or diminish Israel’s failings, but to place them in a context that demands intellectual consistency.”

Weiss, for her part, has always considered herself the self-fashioned poster child of liberal cancel culture run amok, which she laid out in her 1,500-word resignation letter from the New York Times, where she claimed she was the victim of “bullying by colleagues” and an “illiberal environment” while touting her own “bravery” for showing up to work at the paper. (Of course, she had to self-cancel as the Times never actually fired her.)

Amid her “reign of terror,” which has seen her blow up television’s most-watched news show while pushing CBS News further and further to the right, Weiss has also made a series of staffing decisions that have only grown more predictable with each passing month.

After Trump-courting Paramount chief David Ellison installed her last fall, for instance, one of Weiss’s first moves was to bring in close pal Adam Rubenstein as her deputy. Rubenstein, who worked with Weiss at the Times, has claimed he was a victim of “public cancellation” for editing the notorious Tom Cotton 2020 op-ed that sparked the internal backlash that led Weiss to flee the paper. (He would also eventually leave the Times in 2021.)

In a lengthy article for The Atlantic, Rubenstein – whom Weiss has called “her 3 a.m. call” and “second best thing” that happened to her at the Times – insists he was ostracized for liking Chick-fil-A and was dubbed a “heretic” at the paper for his conservative views.

Or take Trevor Phillips, the network’s new global affairs correspondent who Weiss poached from Sky News. While Phillips has had a long career in British politics and broadcasting, he’s also developed a well-earned reputation as an Islamophobe, eventually resulting in his year-plus suspension from the Labour Party.

Nick Bilton, the tech columnist with no broadcast experience who Weiss nonetheless made the head of ‘60 Minutes’ on “Black Thursday,” reportedly had to pass “Bari’s political litmus test” to get the job. Other ex-colleagues noted he’d become disillusioned with the left. “It was the DEI stuff, the campus protests and antisemitism,” one source said.

And, of course, there’s ‘CBS Evening News’ anchor Tony Dokoupil. The former ‘CBS Mornings’ co-host became the face of Weiss’s conservative-friendly makeover of the network, largely thanks to the controversy generated by his combative 2024 Ta-Nehisi Coates interview – which saw Weiss aggressively defend him.

Then we have Peter Attia, the “longevity guru” Weiss hired as an on-air contributor earlier this year, only to discover that he exchanged hundreds of crude messages with deceased sex predator Jeffrey Epstein. Despite that embarrassing revelation, Weiss resisted parting ways with him for weeks, seeing it as capitulating to the “cancel culture” mob. (Attia ultimately resigned in late February.)

“Bari Weiss has no interest in providing space for different viewpoints she doesn’t ideologically agree with,” one CBS staffer told Zeteo last month.

Pesca, whose mission is to help replace the void created by Weiss getting rid of CBS News Radio with an expanded audio podcast product, fits right in with these recent hires and promotions.

According to Defector at the time, Pesca – who is white – “outraged” some Slate staffers during a discussion on the company’s Slack about Donald McNeil leaving the New York Times, after McNeil used a racial slur while chaperoning high school students on a trip. Eventually, Pesca argued that “there are some limited reasons why a non African American journalist” should be able to use the n-word, “when conveying a quote in the name of clarity or factualness.”

Pesca would soon be suspended indefinitely pending an investigation, with Defector noting that current and former Slate staffers said he had “said the n-word at the company office on two separate occasions” in 2019. On top of that, prior to joining Slate, he had used the slur during multiple segments of his NPR podcast – once in 2006 and again in 2007.

One of those segments was titled, “When is the ‘N-Word’ Not a Racial Slur?”

After Pesca’s 2019 incidents, Slate instituted a “sensitive language” policy for podcasts. At an all-hands meeting that year to discuss the new policy, Pesca raised the argument about certain contexts where it should be appropriate for a white person to use the racial slur, noting ‘Slow Burn’ – a black-hosted podcast – had broadcast the word.

“For Black employees, it’s an extremely small ask to not hear that particular slur and not have debate about whether it’s OK for white employees to use that particular slur,” Anderson, who hosted that season of ‘Slow Burn,’ later told the New York Times.

It isn’t just Pesca’s victimization at the hands of the lib media that likely appeals to Weiss. In recent years, he has authored several pieces for The Free Press – such as wondering why Americans are “so nice” to Olympic freestyle skier Eileen Gu or saying “nothing” can be done to address gun violence.

He’s also gone out of his way to gush about Weiss’s management of CBS News, all while taking aim at her critics and other network employees who have taken the former Times opinion columnist to task over her editorial meddling, after she abruptly pulled the “Inside CECOT” story.

In the end, a CBS News reporter told Zeteo that Weiss hiring Pesca was completely unsurprising.

“An absolutely uninspired choice but par for the course. Bari loves anyone who has her shared experiences – particularly when it comes to any kind of kerfuffle even remotely related to the NYT,” the reporter said. “What can you say about choosing someone like Pesca, who argues that in some contexts, white people should be allowed to say the n-word.”

Representatives for CBS News, Pesca, and Weiss did not respond to requests for comment.

Two months after it was abruptly canceled, following a gunman’s breach of security, the White House Correspondents’ Dinner will take place this Friday, this time at the Waldorf Astoria. And, once again, Donald Trump is planning on being there to deliver a speech – which may or may not include the “same rather nasty statements” he had planned for the press last time.

The originally scheduled dinner drew intense criticism for honoring Trump, who has launched a multi-pronged war against the media and the First Amendment, so it is no surprise that Friday’s soiree is also facing heavy scrutiny.

Piggybacking off of their initial April memo urging the White House Correspondents Association to take a stand against Trump’s attacks on the media (and more than just wearing pocket squares), more than 500 veteran journalists – led by former ABC correspondent Lisa Stark – demanded the group “speak truth to power” on Friday. Especially since the administration’s attacks on “press freedom have become even more egregious” these past few weeks.

With the Department of Justice subpoenaing New York Times reporters and their families’ phone records for writing about the president’s Qatari-gifted Air Force One, and Trump threatening to revoke broadcast licenses because networks didn’t carry his recent election-denying primetime speech, the group of journalists called out the WHCA for hosting Trump in the first place.

“We believe it is hypocritical to celebrate the First Amendment in front of the man who relentlessly attacks it,” the organization wrote. “We urge the WHCA to condemn the administration’s actions from the podium and pledge to fight all attempts by his administration to undermine this core pillar of a functioning democracy.”

We asked WHCA Executive Director Steve Thomma and Weijia Jiang, the association’s outgoing president who is organizing the dinner, about the journalist group’s memo and why they are still hosting Trump amid his latest anti-media attacks.
While we did not receive a response, the association provided the following statement to other media outlets regarding the letter: “The White House Correspondents’ Dinner exists because the First Amendment matters most when it’s under pressure. As we continue to celebrate America’s 250th birthday, our choice to gather as journalists, newsmakers and the president in the same room is a reminder of what a free press means to this country – and why it must endure. Not for the media or the president, but for the people who depend on it.”

With the Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery merger suddenly in limbo, now that a federal judge has granted a temporary restraining order to state attorneys general in an antitrust lawsuit, several CNN and CBS News employees I spoke with expressed a sense of relief – even if it could be fleeting.

“This has made my Monday,” a CBS News senior staffer told Zeteo shortly after the merger was halted. “The Ellisons have played this so badly. In my head, I just could not work out why they weren’t more concerned about optics.”

David Ellison and his dad Larry, the Trump-boosting owner of Oracle who recently acquired a large stake in TikTok, have sought to leverage their close ties to the administration to expand their media portfolio. This has seen David go from running the small studio Skydance to owning Paramount and being on the verge of controlling CBS News, CNN, HBO Max, Warner Studios, and a whole array of media products and intellectual properties.

Now that there’s a glimmer of hope that Weiss won’t have a role at CNN and the Ellisons – who have reportedly promised Trump they’d make “sweeping changes” at the network – may not be its new owners, a CNN producer said they were feeling “a bit more optimistic” about the company’s future.

“Very welcome news!” the producer declared. “Now it’s sounding like the delay could kill it. Still seems more likely than not that it’ll go through, so not getting my hopes too high. But any possibility of the deal getting killed will be well received by myself and other CNNers.”

Still, some at CNN are waiting for more concrete news before celebrating. “I’ve stopped getting too invested in the tick tock of events. It’s something every day and could drive you crazy,” one on-air personality said.

A representative for Warner Bros. Discovery declined to comment. We have reached out to Paramount for comment.

Months after Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ helped jumpstart the nation into war with Iran, hosts at the right-wing network are openly begging the president to quickly wrap up the immensely unpopular conflict before it completely annihilates the GOP’s midterm hopes.

“The clock is ticking for President Trump to get out of this war before voters head to the polls. It’s making a lot of Republicans nervous,” Laura Ingraham pleaded on Monday night. “Republicans cannot let this become an endless distraction, especially if they want to win in November.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘ Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

item.title: Platner May Be Out But the Media Insists on Punishing Democrats in Maine
item.description: On this day in 1937, the Senate buried Franklin D. Roosevelt's plan to add seats to the Supreme Court. But his pressure campaign worked, as the court began upholding New Deal laws it once struck down, and a wave of retirements let FDR reshape the bench. Will 2028 Dem presidential candidates learn a…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1937, the Senate buried Franklin D. Roosevelt's plan to add seats to the Supreme Court. But his pressure campaign worked, as the court began upholding New Deal laws it once struck down, and a wave of retirements let FDR reshape the bench. Will 2028 Dem presidential candidates learn a lesson from FDR?

Good morning! It’s Andrew here, reporting once again from the great state of Maine. You may have heard that Maine has a new presumptive Democratic nominee: Troy Jackson. Now that he’s set to face Susan Collins, the Beltway media has some thoughts – and they’re not great.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ reporters aren’t about to let Maine’s Senate contest be about the candidates’ politics, policy views, or records, or even the hell that Donald Trump has unleashed. Plus, we look at the latest development in the Trump administration’s long-running campaign to silence and deport student protester Mohsen Mahdawi.

Democrats in Maine yearn for a different kind of politician in Washington – one who represents their political views and wants, rather than what the D.C. establishment thinks is best for them.

That was the throughline between Maine’s Democratic Senate primary, which saw populist outsider Graham Platner steamroll over the state’s two-term governor, and the conclusion of the hurried process that took place to replace Platner on the ballot after he was accused of rape.

The 17,000 Democrats who participated in the second primary overwhelmingly voted for delegates who support Troy Jackson, the former state Senate president whom Bernie Sanders had backed for governor. The contest wasn’t close. Jackson’s rivals dropped out. Upcoming debates were canceled.

So what was the national media’s response to this impressive organizing display from the new progressive Democratic Senate candidate in Maine?

Within 24 hours of Jackson becoming Maine’s presumptive nominee, three outlets – the Washington Post, CNN, and Politico – all published stories about Jackson having once thrown a bottle of water during an argument over policy when he was leading Maine’s state Senate.

The Post broke the news that Jackson “once angrily threw a water bottle in a meeting after he couldn’t persuade a lawmaker to vote his way.” A few hours later, a second water bottle story hit the race. “In the insular world of Maine politics, it’s known as the ‘water bottle incident’,” CNN wrote. By Tuesday, there was a third, as the New York Times weighed in on “The Water Bottle Episode.”

The three reports relayed that Jackson didn’t always control his temper and had yelled at colleagues while in the legislature. Lawmakers and other sources who spoke to the outlets gave varying accounts of how and where the water bottle was thrown.

Charlotte Warren, one state lawmaker who had argued with Jackson, posted on social media afterwards: “Straight white men are too emotional to be in politics.” She told the Post she’s backing Jackson now, and that they had “hugged it out,” cried, and made up.

“Is this guy going to fight for us? Yes, he is,” Warren said.

Of course, the Jackson water bottle story is a far cry from the series of scandals that dogged Platner – which ranged from his history of misogynist Reddit posts, to his having a Totenkopf-style tattoo inked on his chest for years, to the news he sexted other women after getting married, to the disturbing rape allegation that ended his campaign.

Still, the early Jackson coverage confirms that, even with Platner gone, the national media has little interest in covering this all-important Maine race – which could decide control of the Senate – as anything resembling a contest over the candidates’ competing views, politics, or policy records.

For what it’s worth: Jackson is campaigning in support of Medicare for All, raising taxes on billionaires, a federal right to an abortion, ending Israel’s genocide in Gaza, and abolishing ICE.

The sitting Republican senator, Susan Collins, is… not.

The media would like this race to be another exploration of character – and not both politicians’ character or behavior, to be clear. Never mind that Congress is filled with bad and abusive bosses, bullies, sex pests, and people like Collins who will send your money abroad to kill people without a second thought.

If the Beltway media has its way, this race won’t even be about Donald Trump, the fascist nightmare whom Collins has perpetually enabled and consistently voted with, despite Maine being the only 2026 battleground that Trump lost in the last election.

Trump’s illegal and pointless forever war in Iran, which Collins backed, has sent gas prices soaring.

Collins has repeatedly defended ICE, Trump’s masked and violent secret police, and voted last month to give the agency an extra $70 billion, even after federal agents were videotaped murdering two U.S. citizens in Minnesota.

Last week, an ICE agent – whose family says he is violent and unwell, and should never have been given a gun – shot and killed an immigrant in Biddeford, Maine. An agent was filmed removing the man from his vehicle and letting his lifeless body slam into the ground, where he bled out in front of his wife and young daughter. I went to the scene the next day, and the man’s blood was still there, stained into the road.

When Zeteo’s Prem Thakker asked Collins whether she regretted voting to give ICE more money, she babbled about how the cash was for training and body cameras. Senator Ed Markey (D-Mass.) visited the same ICE facility in Maine where the killer agent worked, and he said ICE told him “that they have no body cameras, and they do no training at this facility.”

It’s no wonder that a new independent poll released Tuesday found Jackson with a three-point lead over Collins, despite 15% of the electorate not knowing much about him.

The University of New Hampshire poll found Collins’ favorability rating is badly underwater: “35% of Mainers have a favorable opinion of her” and “53% have an unfavorable opinion.”

According to the poll, “a plurality rate her as poor or very poor on representing Maine citizens (45%). Slim majorities of Mainers rate Collins as poor or very poor on her votes on key legislation (51%).”

The national media doesn’t seem interested in Collins’ votes on key legislation – just that she’s cast a lot of votes.

Left to centrist reporters and pundits in DC and New York, Maine’s Senate race won’t be about politics, policy, or the candidates’ records – or what its residents want at all.

  • Soldier identified:The U.S. militaryidentifiedSgt. Angel S. Rampersad (28) as the third service member killed in Friday’s attack in Jordan. Rampersad’s death brings the U.S. death toll to 18 service members.
  • Iranian casualties:Over1,700civilians in Iran have been killed since the war started in March. Iran has said that at least 50 people have been killed and 500 injured since the U.S.resumedstrikes this month.
  • Costs of war:Secretary of Defense Pete Hegsethtoldthe Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday that the war with Iran has cost the U.S. $37.5 billion so far.
  • Flights to Lebanon:After meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun, President Trumpsaidthat he will allow the U.S. to resume direct flights to Lebanon – opening up a route that has beensuspendedby the U.S. for over 40 years. Aoun clearlycharmedTrump, with Trump calling him “handsome.”
  • Warning shots:Lebanon’s militaryaccusedIsrael of firing shots at Lebanese soldiers as they began to deploy in a “pilot zone” – an area that Israel is supposed to withdraw from under the U.S.-backed agreement. Israel claimed its military had fired warning shots in the air, because Lebanese forces had left the designated zone.
  • End in sight?According to two sources who spoke toTrump may “at some point” consider dropping his costly Iran war in order to help Republicans avoid humiliation at the ballot box in November.The Atlantic,

The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit has reversed a federal court ruling that freed Columbia student protester Mohsen Mahdawi.

The panel determined that the Vermont judge who freed Mahdawi did not have the authority to intervene in the case before it had moved through the immigration court system.

Let us take you quickly through the shocking series of events:

Last year, the Trump administration kidnapped Mahdawi as part of its campaign to silence pro-Palestine and anti-war free speech. Masked agents detained him at a citizenship interview.

Weeks later, a judge in Vermont freed him, writing that those who know Mahdawi “describe him as a peaceful figure who seeks consensus in a highly-charged political environment” – but that “even if he were a firebrand, his conduct is protected by the First Amendment.”

The administration appealed, while Mahdawi continued navigating his case in immigration court. In February, an immigration judge terminated the removal proceedings against him. Then the administration appealed again, to the Board of Immigration Appeals, which overturned the termination.

The case was then overseen by a different judge (as the initial immigration judge was fired), and in June, the new judge ordered Mahdawi to be removed.

His immigration appeal remains ongoing, and his legal team says that the government has no authority to re-detain him in the meantime. “Federal courts must have the power to step in when the government exploits our country’s immigration system to imprison people for their constitutionally-protected speech,” Luna Droubi, a member of Mahdawi’s legal team, said in a statement. “If the Trump administration can target Mohsen for his advocacy in support of Palestinian rights, they can do it to anyone expressing an opinion they disagree with.”

You can watch Mehdi’s interview with Mahdawi, from March, here.

  • Poison pill:Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortezsaidthat “Republicans are trying to pull a trick this week,” by inserting an unrelated voter suppression measure into a bill to stop insider trading in Congress.
  • Moving along: The Senate Intelligence Committeevotedto advance Jay Clayton’s nomination for national intelligence director, sending it to the floor after he repeatedlyrefusedto acknowledge that Trump lost the 2020 election.
  • Free press:As the White House Correspondents’ Association prepares to host another dinner with Trump this week, over 500 journalistsurgedthe association “to publicly pledge that it will fight back against the Trump admin’s assault on press freedom.”
  • Mass deportation latest:ICE and Border Patrolarrested43,138 people in June, the highest figure since Trump took office again. The administration has nowstartedmoving up immigrants’ hearings with short notice, as an excuse to remove them faster.
  • Money needed:Defense Secretary Pete HegsethsaidCongress must give the Defense Department another $1.5 trillion, calling it “the greatest threat that our nation faces,” even as heacknowledgedthe Pentagon still has $75 billion left over from Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill. That same lawhas cost4.5 million Americans their food assistance.
  • “War criminal”:New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani released avideocalling Benjamin Netanyahu a “war criminal,” while conceding that New York “does not have the independent legal authority” to arrest the Israeli prime minister.
  • Arizona primaries:Progressive Kai Newkirklosthis challenge to Democratic Rep. Greg Stanton in Arizona’s 4th district. In the 1st district primary, Amish Shah led Marlene Galán-Woods, who was backed by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. Alexander Kolodin, whotriedto overturn election results from 2020 and 2022, won the Republican nod for secretary of state.

Who was the first member of the Democratic Socialists of America to become mayor of New York City?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:The Israeli military is building amiles-longearthen barrier that will separate the more than 50% of Gaza it controls from the rest of the strip. Palestinians are rightfully worried that this barrier will soon morph into the new Israeli-enforced border for Gaza, as Israel has already developed a “security zone” around the area.

  • 🇸🇦

    Saudi nuclear deal:Trump approved a “landmark agreement” with Saudi Arabia that will have U.S. companies help the Saudis develop a civilian nuclear program, theWall Street JournalreportedTuesday evening.

  • 🇺🇦

    Another military shakeup:Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskydismissedhis top general Oleksandr Syrskyi, following mass demonstrations over the firing of Ukraine’s defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov. Fedorov was originally fired after clashing with Syrskyi and other generals over war strategy – with Fedorovadvocatingfor the use of drones and robots.

  • 🇮🇳

    Protests in India:On Tuesday, India’s youth-led Cockroach movement vowed tocontinueprotesting India’s education system in New Delhi, despite police brutally attacking them just the day before. Police alsodetainedIndian opposition leader Rahul Gandhi during a sit-in outside Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s residence, with Gandhi calling on Modi to resign.

  • 🇫🇷📵

    France’s Ban:France became thefirstcountry in the European Union to ban children from social media apps like TikTok. The legislation bars children under 15 from using the apps, and comes two years after Australia implemented a similar ban in 2024.

Sir Keir Starmer, who quit as British prime minister on Monday, decided to post this bizarre farewell video to his official TikTok account, with his face and the door to Number 10 Downing Street edited onto… the final scene of ‘Furious 7’ (!)

Zeteo’s Prem Thakker, Lara Strydom, and Andrew McDonald contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Everything You Need to Know About the U.S. Criminal Case Against Nicolás Maduro
item.description: Ousted Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and his wife, former National Assembly President Cilia Flores, are expected back in U.S. court Wednesday as they face criminal prosecution over their alleged participation in a decades-long drug trafficking operation. This hearing comes just six months…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Ousted Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and his wife, former National Assembly President Cilia Flores, are expected back in U.S. court Wednesday as they face criminal prosecution over their alleged participation in a decades-long drug trafficking operation.

This hearing comes just six months after Donald Trump’s administration kidnapped Maduro and Flores in Venezuela as part of an unprecedented regime change operation, setting the stage for a lengthy and complicated legal battle ahead.

But what, exactly, are they accused of? Were their arrests even legal? And why are they being tried by the U.S.?

Here’s what you need to know:

Federal prosecutors allege that, for decades, Maduro abused his positions in government to transport thousands of tons of cocaine to the U.S.

The indictment argues that Maduro “participates in, perpetuates, and protects a culture of corruption in which powerful Venezuelan elites enrich themselves through drug trafficking and the protection of their partner drug traffickers.”

It adds, “The profits of that illegal activity flow to corrupt rank-and-file civilian, military, and intelligence officials, who operate in a patronage system run by those at the top – referred to as the Cartel de Los Soles or Cartel of the Suns.” Technically speaking, this cartel does not exist.

The criminal charges laid out in the indictment include narco-terrorism conspiracy, cocaine importation conspiracy, possession of machine guns and destructive devices, and conspiracy to possess machine guns and destructive devices.

If convicted, several of these charges carry potential life sentences. Maduro has denied the allegations and pleaded not guilty.

Maduro’s capture seemingly violates the Article 2(4) UN Charter, which prohibits a nation from “the threat or use of force against the territorial integrity or political independence of any state.”

However, a 1989 Justice Department memo written by then-Assistant Attorney General Bill Barr concluded that the president has the “inherent constitutional authority” to override international law for U.S. law enforcement operations.

Regardless of the legality of the arrest, Maduro will still have to stand trial. Under the Ker-Frisbie doctrine, defendants in a federal criminal trial are barred from challenging the court’s jurisdiction on the legality of the defendant’s capture or transfer.

The indictment alleges that Cilia Flores “accepted hundreds of thousands of dollars in bribes to broker a meeting between a large-scale drug trafficker and the director of Venezuela’s National Anti-Drug Office.” Following this meeting, Flores allegedly received a cut of every drug flight that arrived safely.

U.S. prosecutors also claim that she helped Maduro maintain state-sponsored gangs to “facilitate and protect” the drug trafficking operation. The two were also accused of having “ordered kidnappings, beatings, and murders against those who owed them drug money or otherwise undermined their drug trafficking operation.”

Flores has been charged with cocaine importation conspiracy, possession of machine guns and destructive devices, and conspiracy to possess machine guns and destructive devices.

Like Maduro, Flores has pleaded not guilty and denies the allegations.

The case is still in its pretrial stage.

Back in March, Maduro and Flores asked the 92-year-old judge overseeing the case, U.S. District Judge Alvin Hellerstein, to dismiss the charges, arguing that the U.S. government’s sanctions are preventing them from paying for their legal representation – a move that could violate their Sixth Amendment right to counsel.

About a month later, the U.S. government agreed to ease some sanctions on Venezuela in order for Maduro and Flores to cover their legal fees.

Judge Hellerstein has yet to issue a formal ruling on the motion to have the case dismissed.

Barry J. Pollack, one of the top criminal defense lawyers in the U.S., will be leading Maduro’s defense. His previous clients include WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange, whom he helped secure a plea deal that led to his release from prison.

Anna Estevao, who represented rapper Sean “Diddy” Combs in his criminal trial last year, also recently joined Maduro’s defense team.

Aside from arguing that his arrest was illegal, Maduro will likely argue that this is a political persecution and that he is entitled to sovereign immunity. Upon pleading not guilty, Maduro reiterated that he is the sitting president of Venezuela, indicating that he can’t be prosecuted in another country’s courts.

Yet, the Justice Department will argue that Maduro is an illegitimate head of state, as the indictment references his “illegally obtained authority.” Even before Maduro’s capture, top administration officials, like U.S. Ambassador to the UN Mike Waltz, claimed that the government does not recognize Maduro as the legitimate leader of Venezuela.

One complication in the government’s argument is that Trump, himself, addressed Maduro as “president” and “leader” of Venezuela when he announced his capture.

The court will have to address this defense before heading to trial, which would take several months.

Maduro and Flores are not the only ones named in the U.S. government’s indictment. The indictment also includes the names of Maduro’s son as well as a current and a former minister, all of whom remain in Venezuela.

U.S. prosecutors accuse Maduro and his co-conspirators of working with FARC (the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia), Tren de Aragua, and the Mexican Sinaloa and Zetas cartels to traffic cocaine.

These cartels were all designated as foreign terrorist organizations by the Trump administration in February 2025.

While this case is extremely complicated, the U.S. government is not in uncharted waters. More than 25 years later, federal prosecutors are relying on the U.S.’s case against Panamanian dictator Manuel Noriega as precedent as they seek to navigate many of the same jurisdictional and constitutional questions raised by prosecuting a foreign leader.

Maduro’s case is expected to take months, if not years, before heading to trial. In that time, Maduro could also potentially secure a plea deal, though it’s worth noting that any agreement reached would ultimately require the court’s approval.

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl contributed to this story.

item.title: Why Is No One Talking About the ‘New War’ Trump Is Waging in Somalia?
item.description: Ask the average person to name the major war that Donald Trump started, and they’ll understandably say Iran, now rounding out its fifth month with no end in sight. But entirely in obscurity, Trump has launched what one think-tank expert terms an “unprecedented increase” in strikes in Somalia – so…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Ask the average person to name the major war that Donald Trump started, and they’ll understandably say Iran, now rounding out its fifth month with no end in sight. But entirely in obscurity, Trump has launched what one think-tank expert terms an “unprecedented increase” in strikes in Somalia – so unprecedented as, in his view, to represent an entirely new war emerging from the U.S. inability to defeat an adversary that it has fought for an entire generation.

It is no longer a surprise that Somalia garners little attention. But it should be scandalous, because such neglect has permitted the U.S. military to wage an adjunct forever-war against jihadists in Somalia for 20 years, ever since a U.S.-backed and long-forgotten Ethiopian invasion in 2006. That ties Somalia with the 2001-21 Afghanistan war as America’s longest foreign conflict. But since the U.S. fights in Somalia without conventional ground forces, it has never faced any pressure to demonstrate success, let alone end operations. The U.S. war in Somalia has become a signal case of the so-called “War on Terror” launching an imperial police action uninterrupted by any need to justify itself. In another six months, this invisible, politically frictionless campaign will rank as the longest foreign war the U.S. has ever fought.

Congress certainly isn’t demanding any justification – or even devoting much in the way of scrutiny.

When the war’s commander, U.S. Africa Command’s Gen. Dagvin Anderson, testified before both the House and Senate armed services committees in May, the legislators charged with military oversight barely bothered to ask. Their attention instead went to Anderson’s testimony-mate, Adm. Brad Cooper of U.S. Central Command, who runs the Iran War.

Anderson and his predecessor, Marine Gen. Michael Langley, have over the past 18 months subjected Somalia to the heaviest bombardment in the war’s history. In 2026 alone, AFRICOM has announced an astonishing 58 strikes, presumed to be mostly drone strikes, and the New America Foundation puts the real total closer to 77. By either accounting, the barrage is greater, in just six months, than the 51 strikes taken by the Biden administration in four years.

The pace AFRICOM is on this year will easily exceed the 96 strikes (when taking into account multiple strikes announced on the same day) it announced launching in 2025. That year represented the previous high-water mark for such strikes on Somalia. Taken together, the second Trump administration has launched between 154 (AFRICOM’s tally) and 203 (New America’s) strikes on the East African coastal nation. That surpasses New America’s total of 192 U.S. strikes on Pakistan in 2010 and 2011, the two most intense years of the U.S. drone war, and with six more months to go. The second Trump administration’s strikes have killed between 190 and 420 Somalis, at least a dozen of them civilians by New America’s tabulations, likely an undercount.

But it’s not exactly right to understand what Trump and Anderson are doing in Somalia as an escalation. According to David Sterman, the deputy director of the future-security program at New America, “We initiated, basically, a new war.”

If this seems familiar, it’s because Trump is, in one sense, picking up in Somalia from where he left off. The first Trump administration also enacted a sharp escalation in drone strikes in Somalia. In 2019 alone, AFRICOM executed 66 strikes, more than the 48 strikes on Somalia it launched during all eight years of the Obama administration. The astonishing spike was only a component of what turned out to be 219 strikes by the time Trump left office.

That volume was made possible by a decision to devolve greater latitude to commanders about when to launch them. “That automatically open[ed] up the aperture,” the former commander of special operations in Africa, Brig. Gen. Donald Bolduc, explained to me at the time. (Yes, the same Bolduc who later ran for Senate in New Hampshire.) Joe Biden required White House approval for requested strikes, as had Barack Obama. Trump reinstated the same command devolution for counterterrorism operations from his first term.

But there’s also been a transformation in Somalia strikes during Trump’s second term. A great deal of them have not been launched against al-Shabaab, the al-Qaeda-aligned insurgency in the Somali south that the U.S. has bombed but never broken for two decades. As Anderson’s testimony indicated, many strikes now target ISIS’s Somali branch in the north.

So far this year, Sterman counts 39 U.S. strikes against al-Shabaab and nearly as many, 33, against ISIS, with another two strikes against unclear targets. (On the phone with me, Sterman counted 38 al-Shabaab strikes, but AFRICOM took another one against the group a few days after we spoke.) William Walldorf, a senior fellow at Defense Priorities, wrote in a July 14 paper that “most” of the second Trump administration’s Somalia strikes have come against ISIS-Somalia, not al-Shabaab.

The fact that ISIS-Somalia exists at all represents a failure of the U.S. war in Somalia. The group broke off from al-Shabaab a decade ago, after al-Shabaab sided with al-Qaeda over ISIS at the peak of the mid-2010s attempted caliphate in western Iraq and eastern Syria. Rather than the split sapping the energy of both, each has persisted, though hardly equally. Sterman observes that ISIS does not have the “history of holding populated territory” that al-Shabaab very much does. Biden took only “two or three strikes and operations” against ISIS in Somalia, Sterman notes. Now, even as Trump has escalated against al-Shabaab, AFRICOM is simultaneously pursuing an al-Shabaab-sized campaign against ISIS, in a different part of the country. That’s why Sterman considers what Trump has done to represent the launching of a new war in Somalia and not just an escalation of the old.

“At least at the strategic level, this ISIS thing is, to me, a significant new decision to wage war against a new enemy,” Sterman says. “It is not just a continuation of the same tactics and approach of what we were doing in Somalia generally.”

Gen. Anderson, who like Bolduc commanded special operations in Africa, has spoken like he wouldn’t mind a third target. While the increase in airstrikes tends “to be what gets a lot of the news” in Somalia, there was another adversary drawing his notice, Anderson said during a February press briefing. “As we see the Houthi threat – smuggling weapons into Somalia, supporting al-Shabaab and the increased cooperation there – we’re looking at ways to increase that domain awareness of our partners so they can protect their borders and help secure those sea lanes and reduce that threat that goes across the Gulf of Aden and the Red Sea,” he said. (I’m so old that I remember when the Yemenis that the U.S. accused al-Shabaab of collaborating with were al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula.)

On the one hand, Anderson, as the commander of an oft-neglected military command, could be attempting to draw in resources devoted to the major conflict in which the U.S. is engaged. Thus far, AFRICOM has not announced any strikes on any Houthi targets. But it also sounds like the groundwork for turning Somalia into a staging area against Iran’s Ansarallah ally, with the Houthis now declaring the gateway to the Red Sea closed to Saudi oil exports like the IRGC has closed the Strait of Hormuz.

More generally, at the May hearing, Anderson indicated to senators that he’d prefer a more assertive posture. He lamented “AFRICOM’s lack of expeditionary capabilities and diminished force posture” and warned it “allows disruptive actors to drive the agenda, undercutting American interests.” The “epicenter of global terrorism,” he warned, “is in Africa.” Walldorf noted drily this week, “AFRICOM is known for overblowing threats.”

If the Somalia wars have been invisible, so have their lasting achievements. The 2026 Worldwide Threat Briefing released by the director of national intelligence tepidly credited U.S. military operations with having “hindered ISIS-Somalia’s ability to pursue attacks against the U.S. and U.S. interests in the region,” they wrote, neglecting to note that ISIS-Somalia has no history of actually attacking the United States. (Neither, outside of Somalia, does al-Shabaab.) However much ISIS-Somalia is presently “hindered,” whatever that means, time has a way of reversing U.S. gains in the War on Terror. In 2022, there was a rare wave of U.S. optimism after Mogadishu’s forces ousted al-Shabaab from central Somalia. But late last month, a report from the International Crisis Group found that al-Shabaab has since “eras[ed] the government’s gains.” And that’s despite the increase in U.S. airstrikes.

Far more enduring is what U.S. strikes do to Somalis who seek simply to survive the long U.S. war. Last month, The Guardian reported on a series of U.S. airstrikes in the southern Somali town of Jamaame on the morning of Nov. 15. When Mohamed Abo Sheikh Ali arrived at the scene of the blast, he saw the bodies of four of his grandchildren. The boys were aged 10, 7, 6 and 4. Beside them was the body of their mother, Safiyo Hassan Abukar. She had been pregnant. Their family were among 12 civilians, eight of them children, killed in the attack, which the paper reported as the single most lethal U.S. strike on civilians in Somalia in 18 years.

The cheapness with which the Trump administration – like its three predecessors – prices Somali life in Somalia mirrors the cheapness with which the Trump administration prices Somali life in America. The deadly swarm of ICE into Minnesota late last year came after demonizing the Somali community there as a criminal one. “I don’t want them in our country. Their country’s no good for a reason,” Trump said in a Dec. 2 White House rant that described Somalis as “garbage” that the country should stop taking in. Weeks before ICE killed Renee Nicole Good and Alex Pretti, its agents were tackling those they presumed to be Somalis into the snow and arresting them regardless of their citizenship. A rideshare driver and U.S. citizen, Ahmed bin Hassan, went viral when ICE, including gas-canister-toting commander Greg Bovino, stopped him based on his accent. In Somalis, MAGA sees a community that represents a trifecta of backgrounds the War on Terror and broader American history have accustomed them to find threatening: Black Muslim immigrants. The violence of ICE mirrors the violence of AFRICOM.

After 20 years, the U.S. war in Somalia is fully normalized, and Somalis casually dehumanized. But it is still astonishing to consider the sheer disinterest in political and congressional attention in the war expanding to a completely new adversary while continuing against the original, undefeated one. Congress at least belatedly passed a war-powers resolution demanding it authorize the Iran war (even if Trump ignored it). It has never authorized any Somalia war. Claims of military authorities derivative from the 2001 Authorization to Use Military Force (AUMF) are stretched beyond the point of absurdity: al-Shabaab did not exist then; it did not have any allegiance to al-Qaeda until six years into the conflict; there is no AUMF against ISIS, which is a competitor to al-Qaeda rather than an ally. None of those circumstances, nor the actual fortunes of the U.S. in Somalia, matter as much as the disinterest shown to a two-decade low-intensity war in East Africa.

“As a strategic, political matter, I think something like that, whether or not it needs a strict new congressional authorization, at least, that should have hit the news and been something publicly discussed,” Sterman says, “rather than something that just emerged out of the policy processes.”

Spencer Ackerman is a Pulitzer Prize and National Magazine Award-winning reporter and the author of Reign of Terror: How The 9/11 Era Destabilized America and Produced Trump. He is also the author of the FOREVER WARS newsletter on Ghost.

item.title: ‘My Father Is a Hero’: Detained Palestinian Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya’s Son Speaks to Zeteo
item.description: Elyas Abu Safiya hasn’t seen his father in person in 19 months. The last time he did, Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya was doing what he had done for most of his life: taking care of other people, saving lives. On Dec. 27, 2024, the Israeli army abducted Dr. Abu Safiya from the Kamal Adwan Hospital – the last…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Elyas Abu Safiya hasn’t seen his father in person in 19 months. The last time he did, Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya was doing what he had done for most of his life: taking care of other people, saving lives.

On Dec. 27, 2024, the Israeli army abducted Dr. Abu Safiya from the Kamal Adwan Hospital – the last functioning hospital in northern Gaza. His son, Ibrahim, was killed in an Israeli bombing in October 2024.

For a week, Israeli authorities denied that they had abducted him. When they finally admitted they were holding him, Israel didn’t disclose his whereabouts for more than a month. He has since been moved around to different prisons, but Israel has shared little other information. We know he’s being held under Israel’s “Unlawful Combatants” Law – a fictitious legal classification (it does not exist in international law) that Israel uses to indefinitely imprison Palestinians without charge or trial. We know he’s been tortured, and has lost an extreme amount of weight – so weak, he is unable to sit upright. During one of his last visits with his lawyer, he said: “They brought me here to kill me. I don’t see myself surviving. This is the end.”

Elyas, helpless, has had to wait for updates from afar – first from Gaza, and now from Kazakhstan, where his family moved late last year. I recently spoke to him about the campaign to release his father, his message to Donald Trump and new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham, and what his dad taught him about loving Palestine. His answers have been translated from Arabic to English and edited for length and clarity below:

What is the latest information that you have on your father?

ELYAS ABU SAFIYA: We know that he is being tortured. We know that he is being held in an underground prison. We know that he is being held without any charges. I do not understand how this can continue to be the case. I do not understand how people who speak about law and human rights can allow my father to be treated this way. And, it isn’t just my father. Yes, I want my father released, but I also want everyone released and especially the other 14 doctors who are imprisoned with him. My father is the doctor who refused to leave his patients behind even though my brother was killed and the Israeli army kept ordering the evacuation of the hospital, including babies and very sick patients. There is no reason to imprison him and no reason to torture him. Being a doctor is not a crime.

This week, Andy Burnham becomes the UK’s new prime minister. He has apologized for the Labour Party’s position on Gaza, stating, “I know many people feel that at the start of Israel’s military action in Gaza my party didn’t get it right and I am sorry about that. The response has too often not been good enough. We need to do better.” How do you feel and what do you think he should do?

ELYAS ABU SAFIYA: I know that the UK Foreign Office has said that they are ‘deeply disturbed’ with Israel’s treatment of my father. We are asking not just the Foreign Office but everyone to intervene in my father’s case because I fear the worst. I think that my father’s breathing his last breaths. Does it make any sense that a physician – who saves lives – is now being beaten with metal batons? His lawyer says that he could not recognize him and that it took about 15 minutes before my father could speak because of the torture and abuse. My father has not received medical care throughout his imprisonment and is being tortured. Why is my father being tortured? My father was kidnapped from inside a hospital while treating patients – some of the most vulnerable people. How is it that he can be abducted and held for more than a year and a half without any charges or without a trial and be subjected to torture? This not only violates international laws, but it violates our basic notions of humanity and morality. It is my understanding that the British were the ones who created this system of ‘administrative detention,’ so they should be the ones who are the most vocal against it.

President Donald Trump declared that there was a “ceasefire” – a term I hate – in Gaza and yet your father remains abducted.

ELYAS ABU SAFIYA: We are asking simply for the right to live. That’s it…the right to live. This is not a huge ask. How is it that kids are not allowed to live? The term ‘ceasefire’ is a misnomer. Palestinian blood has not stopped flowing in Gaza. Not a day goes by where the Israelis are not killing someone. Almost every day there is a new massacre; new deaths, new injured. We just want to see the flow of blood stop. Palestinians are living under catastrophic conditions. There are severe food shortages and shortages of medical supplies. People are living in tents under extreme heat and extreme cold with no future in sight. I feel as though people now view the people of Gaza as deserving of death and not of life. We have been given death sentences.

In one of his interviews, my father asked Trump to intervene, and instead of peace, he sent tanks and bombs.

Your father has become the symbol of both the deliberate destruction of the healthcare system in Gaza as well as Israel’s torture and unlawful imprisonment of Palestinians.

ELYAS ABU SAFIYA: Yes. My father is a hero. He is a role model for all of Palestine for his service, his steadfastness, humanity, and love of Palestine. He always says that Palestine is the most honorable cause, as it is the cause for freedom with its people, particularly in Gaza, sacrificing and enduring the worst, just to be free. All of the doctors need our support because they are all heroes. It is important for us to remember all of them and demand their release.

In response to the campaign to free your father, Israel has now pushed back, claiming that his imprisonment is lawful.

ELYAS ABU SAFIYA: My father has been before several judges in several courts, and each time his file is ‘clean’ [Israel has presented no evidence to the contrary], and yet he continues to be held without any charges. How is this happening? There is no reason to continue to imprison and torture him. If there was any real evidence against my father, why have they not exposed it to the courts or to others? It’s because they do not have evidence. Instead, they make these claims in order to try to justify his imprisonment and torture. My father [has been] an employee of the Palestinian Authority since 1997, first working in Ramallah before he then moved back to Gaza, where he remained a PA employee working in the health sector. He has only ever been an employee of the Palestinian Authority’s Ministry of Health. All physicians and all hospitals are under the direction of the Palestinian Authority. My father was working in the hospital, and the hospital was invaded more than five times. Why was it that it took more than a year, and after five different invasions, that my father was suddenly abducted? My father was a member of MedGlobal, which is a U.S. organization. One of the conditions of his employment was to receive a clean security record from both the Israelis and the Americans – meaning that he cannot be a member of Hamas or any other group. The Israelis cleared him, and they know this.

I know that you are in Kazakhstan now, having arrived just a few months ago with your family. What has that been like?

ELYAS ABU SAFIYA: I have been in Kazakhstan since December with my wife and children. My wife is pregnant with our third child, who, God willing, I will name after my brother who was killed in October 2024. I have mixed feelings about being here. I am part of Gaza and Gaza is part of me. I lived almost all of my life in Gaza. I love Palestine, and I love Gaza. My father raised us to love Palestine and taught us that Palestine needs us, even during its most difficult days. I weep for Gaza. We lived in a tent for nine months, and the images of burned Palestinians and shredded babies tear me apart. It was difficult living through this. I don’t have the words.

At the same time, now living outside of Gaza makes me feel as though I fled hell. I can now speak freely about my father and about the other prisoners. I can speak freely about Gaza. I was personally targeted twice when I was in Gaza, and the Israeli authorities called me and threatened me not to speak about my father. I was threatened all the time, even though all I was asking for was my father’s release. When I was in Gaza, I was afraid to speak out because I knew that they would kill me as they killed so many others speaking the truth, and in particular the journalists.

It has not been easy for us. My father remains imprisoned, and I don’t know when he will be released. My brother was killed. My other brother is still suffering from his injuries. My kids lived through the genocide and starvation, and this affects them and will continue to affect them. We lost everything – literally everything. We lost our house, and my brother and the genocide still follow me because now I see my father being tortured and I feel impotent – unable to do anything – not knowing when my father will be released. I think about my brother all the time and wonder whether his grave remains or whether it was erased. My kids see my father’s picture and are always asking, ‘When will we see Seedo (grandfather)?’ My eldest lived with my father in the hospital, and when she asks about him, I keep saying ‘soon,’ but for the most part I try to protect them by not mentioning him in front of them. All of this weighs on me. But for now I focus on securing my father’s release.

Diana Buttu is a Haifa-based lawyer and analyst who was a legal adviser to the Palestinian negotiating team in the early 2000s and is a frequent commentator and writer on Palestinian and Israeli issues. She is also a practitioner in residence at Georgetown University in Qatar. She writes Zeteo’s ‘A Diary from a Palestinian in Israel.’
