item.title: Bumbling Warmonger Picks Worst Possible Time to Escalate His Iran War
item.description: On this day in 2024, Joe Biden bowed to pressure building since a catastrophic debate against Donald Trump and became the first president since Lyndon B. Johnson in 1968 not to run for re-election. For Democrats, however, it was too late. Good morning, friends; it’s Prem and Swin again. Hope your…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2024, Joe Biden bowed to pressure building since a catastrophic debate against Donald Trump and became the first president since Lyndon B. Johnson in 1968 not to run for re-election. For Democrats, however, it was too late.

Good morning, friends; it’s Prem and Swin again. Hope your air quality is clearer than of late and your lettuce is fresh and safe!

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we have reporting from inside the White House on what exactly is leading Trump to re-escalate the Iran war to even more dangerous levels than before. Plus, the State Department releases a 100-page report on Cuba’s “assault” on the U.S., including a list of Americans whose opinions on the island nation Marco Rubio doesn’t like.

Donald Trump yearned for a ceasefire deal. When it came, it was a losing deal for him, underscoring how desperate he was to get out of his self-made problem. He swallowed it. And things were beginning to alleviate. Oil prices started to go down. But now, mere weeks later, not only has the ceasefire collapsed but the Mad King is drastically ratcheting up his disastrous war once again.

And Trump is doing so as the global economy runs dangerously close to going off the cliff. U.S. oil reserves are at the lowest level since 1983. And while global reserves helped stave off higher price increases previously, those reserves have now been tapped; there is less and less to draw from as the highly unnecessary crisis gets increasingly worse.

And as Trumpland apparently tries to restart negotiations – while threatening more drastic violence – Iran is expressing little interest. To make matters worse, the Houthis are moving to close the Bab-al-Mandeb Strait to Saudi tankers. The blockade would hinder an additional 7% of global oil supply – almost doubling the oil currently reduced by the Iranian blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

All the while, Ukraine has dismantled Russia’s refinery capacity. All to say, the likelihood of global oil shortages has spiked.

This was supposed to be over.

Trump desperately wanted out of his self-made war. After all, he’s a warmonger, but he’s also a “ lazy warmonger.” As we reported mid-June, Trump

In recent days, that’s exactly what has happened. Several administration officials and others close to the White House tell us Trump’s desire to move on from this war has been for the moment quashed by his bloodlust and bruised ego. The president, the sources say, spent too much time consuming media reports and internal government briefings about how the Iranians weren’t taking his latest apocalyptic-sounding threats seriously, and that Tehran believed it had beaten Trump and had all the leverage. The president grew livid at the idea that his bluster was being brushed off, here and abroad, as, well… bluster. So, he decided, he needed to blow shit up again.

“They pissed him off,” one senior Trump official tells us. “So here we go again.”

That sounds simplistic and crude, but it’s where the world is at: to soothe his hurt feelings, Trump is forcing yet more Iranians and Americans to pay with their lives, in a war with no clear mission and no end in sight. But at least the American commander-in-chief got to celebrate at a soccer game.

And now, in response to Iranian attacks killing three more U.S. troops, Trump is taking the world even closer to the edge. On Monday, as conditions spiraled out of control, the U.S. president vowed to retaliate “many times over” for every U.S. soldier killed.

At least 17 U.S. soldiers have been killed and hundreds injured. Zeteo contacted Senators Ted Cruz and John Cornyn of Texas (whose home state saw one soldier killed this weekend) and Senators Thom Tillis and Ted Budd of North Carolina (where another was stationed, at Fort Bragg) to ask what their message might be to Trump, or to the victims’ families, or to the broader U.S. public, as the war’s unpopularity continues to soar. None responded.

Facing his own questions on Monday, Trump was asked about how he first said the war would take a matter of weeks. Trump said his administration was initially “doing a little job of stopping them from having a certain capability.” But now, “we’re just ending it. So it’s really not the same thing.”

He added that, apparently, a week-and-a-half into the war, “we stopped them from ‘probably’” being able to have a nuclear weapon. But, he said, “I don’t want to have the word ‘probably.”

As we reported a month ago, senior U.S. officials were venting to us that President Trump had started another “forever war,” even as he was continuously boasting about the supposed “deal” he was allegedly forging. The way various American officials – including some very sympathetic to Trump and the MAGAfied GOP – see it, Trump has likely started a conflict that will outlast his presidency. Even if Trump gets some kind of “deal” with Iran between now and the midterm elections, the chances remain incredibly high that his administration, the Israeli government, and a future U.S. administration will continue to engage in sporadic bombing of Iran. Because of what Trump and Netanyahu unleashed in February, this is a new status quo, with no end in sight.

The blood won’t be washing off their hands any time soon.

  • Costs of war:The U.S. and Irantradedstrikes, as Donald Trump promised heavy retaliation for the deaths of at least three U.S. troops. Two killed in Jordan werenamed. Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, from Texas, was 19. 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, from Hawaii, was 25. TheNew York Timesreportedthat the Pentagon withheld injury figures. TheWashington PostsaidU.S. intelligence thinks the current strikes will not break Tehran’s resolve.
  • Dire Straits:Iran struck a fourth vessel in the Strait of Hormuz, monitorssaid. Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemensaidthey would block Saudi ships in the Bab al-Mandeb Strait, a move sure to pressure energy markets still further.
  • Price pressure:Gas prices in the U.S.roseto $4 a gallon, last seen in the first months of the war. The White House insisted the war would drive prices down and “put more money back in the pockets of hardworking American families.”
  • Mediation moves:Esmail Baghaei, the Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson, told reporters mediators had sent proposals for de-escalation but did not say what they were. Iran’s interior ministertraveledto Pakistan for talks.
  • Capitol chill:As Republicans considered how to pass $67.1 billion in war funding, Democrats pointed to how both chambers of Congress passed war powers resolutions supposedly reining Trump in. Senator Jack Reedsaid: “I cannot vote to proceed to a bill that enables a war against Iran Congress never authorized.”
  • Lebanon latest:With President Joseph Aoun due to meet Trump, Lebanese troops were set tomoveinto a “pilot zone” in the south of the country, from which Israeli troops were supposed to withdraw. Israel has killed 4,328 and displaced more than a million in its war against Hezbollah since March, local officials say.

Attempting to gin up support for seemingly inevitable action against Havana, Marco Rubio’s State Department published its own Red Scare-esque blacklist, naming American individuals and groups it said helped Cuba “assault” the U.S. “via the novel and insidious method of persuading the children of the West to turn against their own inheritance.”

Introducing a report titled “Cuba: The Capital of 21st Century Communism,” Rubio said: “For more than six decades, the Cuban regime has been the leading sponsor of radical leftism and Third Worldism in the United States. The State Department is exposing the full history of Cuban espionage and subversion in our country. The American people deserve to know.”

Included in the report – alongside a lot of extremely biased history – were the names of left-wing voices or organizations who have supported and visited Cuba, among them “Hasan Piker; Amazon Labor Union founder Christian Smalls; a delegation from the Democratic Socialists of America… and Isra Hirsi, campus activist and daughter of Rep. Ilhan Omar.”

LA Mayor Karen Bass and New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani were named too, as were others including “Amy Goodman, host of ‘Democracy Now!’; Ben Cohen, co-founder of Ben & Jerry’s ice cream; and V, the playwright formerly known as Eve Ensler, who wrote ‘The Vagina Monologues’.” Their original sin? Attending the wedding of Jodie Evans, a co-founder of anti-war group Code Pink, and the left-wing billionaire Neville Roy Singham, now a target of an investigation by federal authorities in New York.

The journalist Ken Klippenstein listed 43 individuals and groups on Rubio’s blacklist. Piker cut to the chase: “Why is the State Department naming American citizens in their Cuba report where they seemingly complain about our opinions on U.S. sanctions policy? This is absolutely protected by the First Amendment. They are trying to chill speech through intimidation.”

  • Polls plummet:Donald Trump’s polling is “the worst this century for a president at this point in his second term,”saidCNN polling guru Harry Enten, adding that for Republican midterm hopes, the “cake… is starting to look at least a little bit baked.” Trump tried an old (and failed) tactic to boost things,announcinga 50% tariff on Canadian goods.
  • Arrest development:TrumpsaidBenjamin Netanyahu “will not be arrested” in New York during the UN General Assembly in September. Mayor Zohran Mamdanifirst toldhe would consider such a step, to hold the Israeli PM to account for war crimes. Netanyahu isZeteosubjectto an International Criminal Court warrant.
  • Fire over ICE:In New York, a manseta blaze, let off fireworks and waved an air rifle outside an ICE office. In DC, Trump border czar Tom Homancomplainedabout “hateful rhetoric” against ICE agents and said “there will be bloodshed” if it doesn’t stop. There has already been bloodshed, not least in deadly shootings by ICE in Texas and Maine this month and the killings of two U.S. citizens in Minnesota earlier this year.
  • Paper chase:The Trump administrationsoughtphone records ofNew York Timesjournalists and their relatives over reporting on the Qatari-gifted Air Force One lacking security features. A judgepausedenforcement of the subpoenas.
  • Maine man:A Democratic U.S. Senate debate in the race to face Republican Susan Collins in Maine wasscrappedafter progressive Troy Jackson became the presumptive nominee. TheWashington Postreportedthat as state senate president, Jackson “once angrily threw a water bottle.” CNN filed asimilar article.
  • States of play:Senator Elizabeth WarrenbackedAbdul El-Sayed, the progressive Senate candidate in Michigan. Filingsshowedrival Rep. Haley Stevens benefited from $11 million in donations by the corporate front group Center Forward. In South Carolina, MAGA publicity hound Nancy Macesaidshe would not enter the race to fill the late Lindsey Graham’s Senate seat, after all.
  • Merger pause:A judge paused Paramount’s takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery, parent of CNN, to consider a challenge filed by 12 states. California AG Rob Bontasaluted“a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” adding: “We have a full tank of gas [and] the law on our side.”

Andy Burnham is the first British prime minister from which left-wing party?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Hamaselecteda new leader, Khalil al-Hayya, until now a member of a five-man leadership council with links to Iran and involved in talks with the U.S. last year. Four of al-Hayya’s sons have been killed by Israel.
  • 🇮🇱 Too little, too late:Former U.S. national security adviser Jake SullivantoldtheNew Yorkerthe Biden administration considered withholding military aid to stop Israel’s genocide against the Palestinians, only to “always defer… thinking that we were close to getting a deal.” “We should have put more pressure on Israel,” Sullivan said.

  • 🇬🇧

    Next up:Andy Burnham took office as the UK’s seventh prime minister in 10 years since Brexit. Offering a 10-year plan to restore stability and revive the economy, hepromised“a new political model and a new economic model,” based on reversing the mistakes of the brutal Thatcherite 1980s.
  • 🇲🇽 Saying sorry:Sentenced in New York to life in prison, Mexican drug kingpin Ismael “El Mayo” Zambada, 76,saidviolence he fostered for three decades “must end.” He also apologized “for the harm I caused and the example I set.”

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:The death toll from the rare Bundibugyo virus in DR Congo rose to at least 930 out of 2,344 cases with health workers among the dead, authoritiessaid, in the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record.

It says something for curiously thatched North Carolina Republican and top Democratic midterms target Rep. Chuck Edwards that after the revelation he gave female staffers blue and pink handguns, this Axios report on the extent of his questionable behavior toward such women manages to get even more bizarre.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Bumbling Warmonger Picks Worst Possible Time to Escalate His Iran War
item.description: On this day in 2024, Joe Biden bowed to pressure building since a catastrophic debate against Donald Trump and became the first president since Lyndon B. Johnson in 1968 not to run for re-election. For Democrats, however, it was too late. Good morning, friends; it’s Prem and Swin again. Hope your…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2024, Joe Biden bowed to pressure building since a catastrophic debate against Donald Trump and became the first president since Lyndon B. Johnson in 1968 not to run for re-election. For Democrats, however, it was too late.

Good morning, friends; it’s Prem and Swin again. Hope your air quality is clearer than of late and your lettuce is fresh and safe!

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we have reporting from inside the White House on what exactly is leading Trump to re-escalate the Iran war to even more dangerous levels than before. Plus, the State Department releases a 100-page report on Cuba’s “assault” on the U.S., including a list of Americans whose opinions on the island nation Marco Rubio doesn’t like.

Donald Trump yearned for a ceasefire deal. When it came, it was a losing deal for him, underscoring how desperate he was to get out of his self-made problem. He swallowed it. And things were beginning to alleviate. Oil prices started to go down. But now, mere weeks later, not only has the ceasefire collapsed but the Mad King is drastically ratcheting up his disastrous war once again.

And Trump is doing so as the global economy runs dangerously close to going off the cliff. U.S. oil reserves are at the lowest level since 1983. And while global reserves helped stave off higher price increases previously, those reserves have now been tapped; there is less and less to draw from as the highly unnecessary crisis gets increasingly worse.

And as Trumpland apparently tries to restart negotiations – while threatening more drastic violence – Iran is expressing little interest. To make matters worse, the Houthis are moving to close the Bab-al-Mandeb Strait to Saudi tankers. The blockade would hinder an additional 7% of global oil supply – almost doubling the oil currently reduced by the Iranian blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

All the while, Ukraine has dismantled Russia’s refinery capacity. All to say, the likelihood of global oil shortages has spiked.

This was supposed to be over.

Trump desperately wanted out of his self-made war. After all, he’s a warmonger, but he’s also a “ lazy warmonger.” As we reported mid-June, Trump

In recent days, that’s exactly what has happened. Several administration officials and others close to the White House tell us Trump’s desire to move on from this war has been for the moment quashed by his bloodlust and bruised ego. The president, the sources say, spent too much time consuming media reports and internal government briefings about how the Iranians weren’t taking his latest apocalyptic-sounding threats seriously, and that Tehran believed it had beaten Trump and had all the leverage. The president grew livid at the idea that his bluster was being brushed off, here and abroad, as, well… bluster. So, he decided, he needed to blow shit up again.

“They pissed him off,” one senior Trump official tells us. “So here we go again.”

That sounds simplistic and crude, but it’s where the world is at: to soothe his hurt feelings, Trump is forcing yet more Iranians and Americans to pay with their lives, in a war with no clear mission and no end in sight. But at least the American commander-in-chief got to celebrate at a soccer game.

And now, in response to Iranian attacks killing three more U.S. troops, Trump is taking the world even closer to the edge. On Monday, as conditions spiraled out of control, the U.S. president vowed to retaliate “many times over” for every U.S. soldier killed.

At least 17 U.S. soldiers have been killed and hundreds injured. Zeteo contacted Senators Ted Cruz and John Cornyn of Texas (whose home state saw one soldier killed this weekend) and Senators Thom Tillis and Ted Budd of North Carolina (where another was stationed, at Fort Bragg) to ask what their message might be to Trump, or to the victims’ families, or to the broader U.S. public, as the war’s unpopularity continues to soar. None responded.

Facing his own questions on Monday, Trump was asked about how he first said the war would take a matter of weeks. Trump said his administration was initially “doing a little job of stopping them from having a certain capability.” But now, “we’re just ending it. So it’s really not the same thing.”

He added that, apparently, a week-and-a-half into the war, “we stopped them from ‘probably’” being able to have a nuclear weapon. But, he said, “I don’t want to have the word ‘probably.”

As we reported a month ago, senior U.S. officials were venting to us that President Trump had started another “forever war,” even as he was continuously boasting about the supposed “deal” he was allegedly forging. The way various American officials – including some very sympathetic to Trump and the MAGAfied GOP – see it, Trump has likely started a conflict that will outlast his presidency. Even if Trump gets some kind of “deal” with Iran between now and the midterm elections, the chances remain incredibly high that his administration, the Israeli government, and a future U.S. administration will continue to engage in sporadic bombing of Iran. Because of what Trump and Netanyahu unleashed in February, this is a new status quo, with no end in sight.

The blood won’t be washing off their hands any time soon.

  • Costs of war:The U.S. and Irantradedstrikes, as Donald Trump promised heavy retaliation for the deaths of at least three U.S. troops. Two killed in Jordan werenamed. Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, from Texas, was 19. 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, from Hawaii, was 25. TheNew York Timesreportedthat the Pentagon withheld injury figures. TheWashington PostsaidU.S. intelligence thinks the current strikes will not break Tehran’s resolve.
  • Dire Straits:Iran struck a fourth vessel in the Strait of Hormuz, monitorssaid. Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemensaidthey would block Saudi ships in the Bab al-Mandeb Strait, a move sure to pressure energy markets still further.
  • Price pressure:Gas prices in the U.S.roseto $4 a gallon, last seen in the first months of the war. The White House insisted the war would drive prices down and “put more money back in the pockets of hardworking American families.”
  • Mediation moves:Esmail Baghaei, the Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson, told reporters mediators had sent proposals for de-escalation but did not say what they were. Iran’s interior ministertraveledto Pakistan for talks.
  • Capitol chill:As Republicans considered how to pass $67.1 billion in war funding, Democrats pointed to how both chambers of Congress passed war powers resolutions supposedly reining Trump in. Senator Jack Reedsaid: “I cannot vote to proceed to a bill that enables a war against Iran Congress never authorized.”
  • Lebanon latest:With President Joseph Aoun due to meet Trump, Lebanese troops were set tomoveinto a “pilot zone” in the south of the country, from which Israeli troops were supposed to withdraw. Israel has killed 4,328 and displaced more than a million in its war against Hezbollah since March, local officials say.

Attempting to gin up support for seemingly inevitable action against Havana, Marco Rubio’s State Department published its own Red Scare-esque blacklist, naming American individuals and groups it said helped Cuba “assault” the U.S. “via the novel and insidious method of persuading the children of the West to turn against their own inheritance.”

Introducing a report titled “Cuba: The Capital of 21st Century Communism,” Rubio said: “For more than six decades, the Cuban regime has been the leading sponsor of radical leftism and Third Worldism in the United States. The State Department is exposing the full history of Cuban espionage and subversion in our country. The American people deserve to know.”

Included in the report – alongside a lot of extremely biased history – were the names of left-wing voices or organizations who have supported and visited Cuba, among them “Hasan Piker; Amazon Labor Union founder Christian Smalls; a delegation from the Democratic Socialists of America… and Isra Hirsi, campus activist and daughter of Rep. Ilhan Omar.”

LA Mayor Karen Bass and New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani were named too, as were others including “Amy Goodman, host of ‘Democracy Now!’; Ben Cohen, co-founder of Ben & Jerry’s ice cream; and V, the playwright formerly known as Eve Ensler, who wrote ‘The Vagina Monologues’.” Their original sin? Attending the wedding of Jodie Evans, a co-founder of anti-war group Code Pink, and the left-wing billionaire Neville Roy Singham, now a target of an investigation by federal authorities in New York.

The journalist Ken Klippenstein listed 43 individuals and groups on Rubio’s blacklist. Piker cut to the chase: “Why is the State Department naming American citizens in their Cuba report where they seemingly complain about our opinions on U.S. sanctions policy? This is absolutely protected by the First Amendment. They are trying to chill speech through intimidation.”

  • Polls plummet:Donald Trump’s polling is “the worst this century for a president at this point in his second term,”saidCNN polling guru Harry Enten, adding that for Republican midterm hopes, the “cake… is starting to look at least a little bit baked.” Trump tried an old (and failed) tactic to boost things,announcinga 50% tariff on Canadian goods.
  • Arrest development:TrumpsaidBenjamin Netanyahu “will not be arrested” in New York during the UN General Assembly in September. Mayor Zohran Mamdanifirst toldhe would consider such a step, to hold the Israeli PM to account for war crimes. Netanyahu isZeteosubjectto an International Criminal Court warrant.
  • Fire over ICE:In New York, a manseta blaze, let off fireworks and waved an air rifle outside an ICE office. In DC, Trump border czar Tom Homancomplainedabout “hateful rhetoric” against ICE agents and said “there will be bloodshed” if it doesn’t stop. There has already been bloodshed, not least in deadly shootings by ICE in Texas and Maine this month and the killings of two U.S. citizens in Minnesota earlier this year.
  • Paper chase:The Trump administrationsoughtphone records ofNew York Timesjournalists and their relatives over reporting on the Qatari-gifted Air Force One lacking security features. A judgepausedenforcement of the subpoenas.
  • Maine man:A Democratic U.S. Senate debate in the race to face Republican Susan Collins in Maine wasscrappedafter progressive Troy Jackson became the presumptive nominee. TheWashington Postreportedthat as state senate president, Jackson “once angrily threw a water bottle.” CNN filed asimilar article.
  • States of play:Senator Elizabeth WarrenbackedAbdul El-Sayed, the progressive Senate candidate in Michigan. Filingsshowedrival Rep. Haley Stevens benefited from $11 million in donations by the corporate front group Center Forward. In South Carolina, MAGA publicity hound Nancy Macesaidshe would not enter the race to fill the late Lindsey Graham’s Senate seat, after all.
  • Merger pause:A judge paused Paramount’s takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery, parent of CNN, to consider a challenge filed by 12 states. California AG Rob Bontasaluted“a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” adding: “We have a full tank of gas [and] the law on our side.”

Andy Burnham is the first British prime minister from which left-wing party?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Hamaselecteda new leader, Khalil al-Hayya, until now a member of a five-man leadership council with links to Iran and involved in talks with the U.S. last year. Four of al-Hayya’s sons have been killed by Israel.
  • 🇮🇱 Too little, too late:Former U.S. national security adviser Jake SullivantoldtheNew Yorkerthe Biden administration considered withholding military aid to stop Israel’s genocide against the Palestinians, only to “always defer… thinking that we were close to getting a deal.” “We should have put more pressure on Israel,” Sullivan said.

  • 🇬🇧

    Next up:Andy Burnham took office as the UK’s seventh prime minister in 10 years since Brexit. Offering a 10-year plan to restore stability and revive the economy, hepromised“a new political model and a new economic model,” based on reversing the mistakes of the brutal Thatcherite 1980s.
  • 🇲🇽 Saying sorry:Sentenced in New York to life in prison, Mexican drug kingpin Ismael “El Mayo” Zambada, 76,saidviolence he fostered for three decades “must end.” He also apologized “for the harm I caused and the example I set.”

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:The death toll from the rare Bundibugyo virus in DR Congo rose to at least 930 out of 2,344 cases with health workers among the dead, authoritiessaid, in the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record.

It says something for curiously thatched North Carolina Republican and top Democratic midterms target Rep. Chuck Edwards that after the revelation he gave female staffers blue and pink handguns, this Axios report on the extent of his questionable behavior toward such women manages to get even more bizarre.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: 7 Things You Need to Know About the Bab al-Mandeb Strait - That Trump Probably Doesn't
item.description: Donald Trump’s war in Iran was supposed to be winding down. It is not – and things could get much worse soon, with news that Iran’s allies are planning to close the Bab al-Mandeb Strait to the Saudis. In recent days, Trump has expanded strikes to target civilian infrastructure inside Iran . Iran…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump’s war in Iran was supposed to be winding down. It is not – and things could get much worse soon, with news that Iran’s allies are planning to close the Bab al-Mandeb Strait to the Saudis.

In recent days, Trump has expanded strikes to target civilian infrastructure inside Iran. Iran has answered by targeting Gulf infrastructure and formally exiting the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding, a development that Trump said he “couldn’t care less” about.

Now, Tehran’s allies in Yemen have opened a second front at sea on the other side of the Arabian Peninsula. On Monday, the Houthis – the Iran-backed rebel group that controls northern Yemen, including its coastline above the Bab al-Mandeb Strait – declared a maritime embargo against U.S. ally Saudi Arabia “effective immediately,” asserting that the Bab al-Mandeb is now closed to Saudi maritime traffic.

If the Houthis close the Bab al-Mandeb Strait to the Saudis, and the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, it could send oil prices soaring to new levels.

Here are 7 things you really should know about the Bab al-Mandeb Strait that could dominate our headlines in the coming days…

Bab al-Mandeb translates to “Gate of Tears” or “Gate of Grief” – bab is Arabic for gate or door, mandab for lamentation. The strait is a narrow, cliff-lined passage of strong currents, shifting winds, and reef-strewn channels that has been wrecking ships since antiquity. One folk etymology, recorded in the Encyclopædia Britannica as far back as its 1878 edition, holds that the name mourns the multitudes drowned in an ancient cataclysm that tore Asia and Africa apart.

The modern hazards are worse. During Yemen’s civil war, the Houthis scattered hundreds of naval mines along the coast up to Bab al-Mandeb – a UN panel found 44 in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden in 2017 alone, four of which detonated on commercial vessels, and that March, a mine killed two sailors aboard a Yemeni Coast Guard boat. Drifting mines still kill Yemeni fishermen today.

The Bab al-Mandeb separates Yemen from the Horn of Africa, connecting the Red Sea, the Gulf of Aden, and the Indian Ocean. Djibouti and Eritrea border it on the west; Yemen – including its Houthi-controlled coastline lies on the eastern edge. At its narrowest, the strait is just 18 miles wide.

The world’s naval powers have long understood what this doorway is worth. Djibouti, directly across from Yemen, hosts Camp Lemonnier, the only permanent U.S. military base in Africa, with more than 4,000 personnel, plus China’s first-ever overseas military base seven miles away, alongside French and Japanese installations.

Roughly 12% of global trade passes through Bab al-Mandeb daily, including heavily laden container ships traveling between Asia and Europe – about a quarter of the world’s container traffic.

The oil numbers, though, have dramatically increased since the Iranians closed the Strait of Hormuz. In the first quarter of 2026, 5.4 million barrels of crude oil and petroleum transited through Bab al-Mandeb. By June, total petroleum flows hit 7.4 million barrels per day – about 7% of global output, and up more than 75% from a year earlier – because Saudi Arabia now pumps roughly 70% of its energy exports through the East-West pipeline to the Red Sea port of Yanbu. A full closure would strand most Saudi exports inside the region, and cut global oil supply by roughly another 7%.

Formally, they haven’t – but they’ve come close. After Oct. 7, 2023, the Houthis began attacking ships around the strait in solidarity with Palestine, conducting more than 100 attacks on commercial vessels, beginning with the seizure of the Galaxy Leader in November 2023. Four ships were sunk, one seized, and at least eight seafarers killed. The attacks stopped following the Trump-negotiated “ceasefire” between Israel and Hamas.

The Houthi campaign never formally closed the strait, but it didn’t need to. Daily transit volume fell 57% as shipping fled around Africa due to their actions.

The embargo is the latest turn in Yemen’s decade-long civil war, a proxy war pitting the Iranian-backed Houthis against a Saudi-led coalition, with the fighting largely frozen since 2022. They have fought over control of the Bab al-Mandeb before; in 2018, Saudi Aramco temporarily halted oil shipments through the strait after Houthi forces attacked two of its tankers.

A week ago, Saudi Arabia bombed Sanaa’s airport to block a flight from Tehran carrying a Houthi delegation. The Houthis answered with missile fire at Saudi Arabia – and, now, an embargo of the Bab al-Mandeb Strait.

The Bab al-Mandeb is also part of Iran’s wider standoff with Washington. Three sources recently told Reuters that Iran had told the Houthis to be ready to close the Red Sea if Trump follows through on his threat to strike Iran’s power grid, with drones and missiles already positioned in the highlands above the strait.

U.S. gas prices hit $4 a gallon again on Monday, and now there’s the compounding risk that if both Bab al-Mandeb and Hormuz remain closed, roughly 25% of global oil and gas supplies could be affected.

Unlike with the Strait of Hormuz, there is no land bypass for cargo forced out of the Red Sea. A senior Houthi official warned that closing both straits would push oil to $200 a barrel.

Outside of the oil market, an estimated 17% of global internet traffic runs through undersea cables at the strait. In 2024, the Rubymar – a cargo ship left sinking by a Houthi missile strike – dragged its anchor across the seabed and severed three of those cables, disrupting internet traffic between Asia and Europe.

Two Western naval coalitions patrolled these waters during the last Houthi campaign, but the attacks still continued. Trump’s own months-long 2025 bombing campaign against the Houthis ended with the group still standing, operational and shooting.

Even after years of sanctions and airstrikes, the Houthis remain fully capable of throttling the strait – because they don’t need to close it entirely.

Repeated attacks, or even the credible threats of attacks, are enough to push shipping lines back around Africa, causing a detour of up to two weeks per voyage that, during the last Houthi campaign, cost the global container fleet an estimated $7-9 billion a year in added shipping expenses.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald, Mohammed Abdelbary, and Randa Jumean contributed to this piece.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: The Arrested Tate Brothers Are Tied to the Trumps: Why Won’t Dems Exploit This?
item.description: ⚽ The World Cup may be over, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders! Good…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
⚽ The World Cup may be over, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders!

Good morning, and Happy Monday again, and congratulations to Spain. Martin here, in Washington, DC, delighted as ever to be bringing you the news you need to know to start your week.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we look at the dramatic arrest in Florida of the Anglo-American brothers Andrew and Tristan Tate, wanted on a raft of serious charges in the UK, and ask why top Democrats don’t seem interested in making clear how close the Tates have grown to the Trump family and the Republican Party they own. We also consider the latest catastrophic re-escalation of Donald Trump’s illegal war with Iran, and consider Troy Jackson’s barnstorming display in Maine, where he stands on the brink of confirmation as the Democratic U.S. Senate nominee.

In Miami on Saturday, U.S. marshals arrested the Anglo-American manosphere influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate. In the UK, the brothers face a total of 59 charges, covering rape, sexual assault, trafficking, assault, and offenses related to indecent images of a child and extreme pornography.

Matt Jury, a lawyer who represents several alleged British victims of Andrew Tate, hailed “the real prospect of extradition… after years of public campaigning by the four British women I represent,” adding: “Andrew and Tristan Tate are accused of some of the most serious offenses. It is time they face justice.”

For the brothers, lawyer Joseph McBride said: “The world knows Andrew and Tristan Tate are innocent. Their enemies know it best of all. That is exactly why they have been attacked. We are confident that once a competent judge sees the facts, and once the Department of Justice confronts this egregious abuse of its own authority, Andrew and Tristan Tate will walk free. America does not do Britain’s political dirty work.”

But plenty of Americans have done political dirty work on behalf of the Tates and it seems incredible, particularly in light of ongoing scandal over sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and his links to Donald Trump, that senior Democrats with national platforms and profiles did not this weekend seize on connections between the Tates, the Trumps, and the Republican Party, in order to flood the zone with comment and questions, and shine a harsh spotlight firmly the way of the White House.

It’s not as if material is lacking.

Consider the dreadful account provided just last month by Heidi Blake’s shattering New Yorker exposé. Noting that Andrew Tate has long been “an enthusiastic supporter of Donald Trump” – with Tate saying, “He’s grabbing bitches by the pussy, I like that guy” – Blake tied the Tates and the Trumps firmly together, not least in examining how President Trump’s special envoy, Ric Grenell, appeared last year to secure their freedom to come to the U.S. from Romania, where they face further serious charges.

In 2016, via Donald Trump Jr., Andrew Tate secured an invitation to Trump Tower, getting a photo with the president-elect and bragging about “access to the president’s son.” McBride, now the Tates’ lawyer, was a friend of Trump Jr. too. Later, Blake noted, Tate made a deal with the right-wing streaming service Rumble, where investors included JD Vance, Donald Trump’s future vice-president, and tech mogul Peter Thiel, Vance’s ideological sponsor. In December 2022, when the Tates were arrested in Romania on sex-trafficking charges, Donald Trump Jr. called the case “absolute insanity” and defended them in public.

MAGA influencers from Tucker Carlson to Charlie Kirk praised the Tates too. In the run-up to the 2024 election, Andrew Tate developed relationships with Trump Jr. and with Barron Trump, the president’s youngest son. After Donald Trump survived an assassination attempt in Pennsylvania, Andrew Tate later told reporters: “I’m very close with the Trump family. I know them well. I spoke to Barron after the incident. I look forward to once I’m free being with Donald Trump in person, and reminding him that he’s a bulletproof badass.”

In the weeks before election day, Blake wrote, Tate “pumped out pro-MAGA content, mobilizing young men in such large numbers that Kamala Harris later named him as a key factor in her defeat. When the result was in, Tate posted jubilantly on X: ‘THE PATRIARCHY IS BACK.’ After the inauguration, he added, ‘The Tates will be free, Trump is the president.’”

Vance followed the Tates on X. A young Tate lawyer, Paul Ingrassia, was picked as White House liaison to the DOJ. Alina Habba, the New Jersey parking garage lawyer who became a close Trump aide, appeared on a podcast with Andrew Tate, telling him she was a “big fan” and comparing his legal travails to those of President Trump (who was found liable in a civil trial for sexual abuse). When the Tates were allowed to travel to the U.S., longtime Trump adviser Roger Stone celebrated online.

In February 2025, the brothers arrived in Florida. According to ProPublica, the White House intervened to return electronic devices seized when the Tates landed. After Andrew Tate left the country, he was accused of sexual assault. When the Epstein files were released, Tate disparaged the victims. In Las Vegas and Miami, the brothers attended UFC fights. CEO Dana White, a close Trump ally, offered personal greetings.

Florida Governor Ron DeSantis has spoken against the Tates and announced a criminal investigation. Regardless, they have cultivated GOP ties. Last week, the brothers were in DC. According to the Daily Mail, they were guests of Paolo Zampolli, another Trump special envoy who says that in his days as a modeling agency boss (and Epstein associate) he introduced Trump to Melania Knauss. Zampolli told the Mail the Tates were in town for

The Mail also published a picture of the brothers on Capitol Hill with MAGA Texas Rep. Wesley Hunt, in his congressional office. The Mail quoted a Hunt source as saying, “Wesley was beyond excited to meet with them.” A spokesman for the Tates said: “Washington knows that Andrew and Tristan Tate’s ability to influence the next election is unmatched. Republicans and Democrats love them. Both parties are inviting them to all kinds of interesting events because both parties understand the same thing: the Tate brothers command the loyalty of millions of young men and a growing number of women. All of them vote.’”

Then, on Saturday, the U.S. Marshals swooped. The Tates are due in federal court in Miami next week. Surely – surely – Democrats must now realize that voters need to know more about the web of ties between these accused rapists and traffickers and the president’s family. To reach for the obvious comparison, imagine if Joe Biden was still in office and his son, Hunter, was revealed to be close to two men accused of sexual assault and trafficking. Would the GOP hold back?

To use the terminology of another Anglo import now leaving U.S. shores – the World Cup – it’s a glaring open goal. Are the Democrats really going to miss this one, too?

  • U.S. deaths:At least three troops werekilled, including one in Iraq during the disposal of an Iranian drone, the Pentagon said, a day after Iranian missile strikes on Jordan killed two. Remains recovered there were believed to be those of a soldier first declared missing. The U.S. death toll stood at 17, the wounded at more than 420.
  • Iranian casualties:The Iranian Health Ministrysaidat least 50 people had been killed and 517 wounded since the war restarted earlier this month.
  • Intense exchanges:U.S. and Iranian strikes continued into Monday, both sides targeting critical infrastructure. Iran said a construction site for a nuclear power plant was hit. The IAEAsaidthe site contained no nuclear material when it was last inspected.
  • Slow Strait:Monitorssaidtraffic in the Strait of Hormuz remained low, despite U.S. Central Commandsayingit was “degrad[ing] Iran’s ability to threaten commercial shipping.” Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard said two vessels were in an “accident.” A British military monitorreporteda ship ablaze off Oman.
  • Lebanon latest:President Joseph AounmetSecretary of State Marco Rubio ahead of a White House meeting on Tuesday. At issue: how to implement an agreement leading to Israeli withdrawals from the south of the country.

Former Maine Senate president Troy Jackson is on the brink of securing the Democratic nomination for U.S. Senate, to replace Graham Platner against Republican incumbent Susan Collins.

Jackson, a logger from the north of the state who was third in the primary for governor, dominated Saturday caucuses staged after Platner, a populist progressive who dominated the Senate primary, pulled out over allegations of sexual assault.

Voting continued Sunday, and the nominee will not be made official until next Saturday, but the choice was all but made after Democrats overwhelmingly voted to send Jackson’s slate of delegates to the party’s convention.

Jackson’s major rivals dropped out. Secretary of State Shenna Bellows saluted “every volunteer who worked around the clock for this movement” and said: “That energy is exactly what we need to beat Susan Collins in November – and Democrats don’t have a day to waste in unifying around that shared goal.” Nirav Shah said Maine Democrats were “united, angry, and energized.”

Collins, seeking a sixth six-year term, is the only Republican running for re-election in a state won by Kamala Harris in 2024.

Jackson, who had Bernie Sanders’s endorsement for governor, appealed to Platner supporters. In Portland on Friday, he said: “A lot of people… believed that Maine could send a real working-class fighter to the United States Senate, and that hope does not belong to one person. That movement does not disappear.” A short statement Saturday echoed Platner’s tone, saying: “I’ve been getting text messages all day about, you know, what a great job I did, and I didn’t do a goddamn thing. All of you just smoked it… We’re well on our way to getting the government we fucking deserve. And that’s what I’m excited about.”

A Jackson supporter, Travis Jones of Seal Harbor, told Politico he “was a big supporter of… Platner. I really liked his policies. Troy mirrors a lot of those. The previous campaign really energized me, and here I am today.”

  • Goodbye World Cup:104 matches across three countries over five weeks. That was the 2026 World Cup, which ended on Sundayin New Jersey at MetLife Stadium, as Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in the final and became champions for the second time. Argentina, down to 10 men after a red card, became the first team in World Cup history to fail to record a single shot on target during regular time in a final. Donald Trump, meanwhile, became the first U.S. president ever to attend a World Cup final, where he wasbooed,appearedto fall asleep, and… well… scroll down to see what he did at the end.Sigh.
  • ICE chills:ICE shared Medicaid data with Palantir, the tech firm run by rightwinger Peter Thiel, filingsshowed. TheNew York Timesreportedthat federal agents have been told the FBI will no longer investigate confrontations with immigration agents,and describeda Chicago U.S. attorney’s office in chaos overfailedimmigration cases. The federal governmentactivatedan “alien terrorist” deportation court, created in 1996 but not used.
  • ‘War criminal’:New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani said he was reviewing whether his administration could arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu during the UN General Assembly in September. “I believe that Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” MamdanitoldtheNew York Times. “He’s a war criminal.” Lest we forget, Mamdani first made his much-discussed pledge to arrest Netanyahu in aninterviewwithZeteoandMehdiin December 2024.
  • Israeli effort:Israel has a $45 million contract with former Trump campaign chief Brad Parscale, for an attempt to arrest a trend against Israel in U.S. public opinion, theWall Street Journalreported. In Michigan, Democratic Rep. Haley Stevensavoidedcomment on her $30 million support by AIPAC as she attempted to win a Senate seat. Progressive candidate Abdul El-Sayed wasattackedby Pennsylvania Democratic (in name only) Senator John Fetterman – in part, for being a Muslim.
  • Place your bets:Florida GOP Rep. Anna Paulina Luna tipped off MAGA influencer Rogan “DC Draino” O’Handley about the impending selection of JD Vance as Trump’s running mate in 2024, allowing him to place a winning bet, theWSJreported. Luna denied saying she had done so.
  • Run ends:Theinsurgentindependent Austin Ahlmanendedhis campaign for a Nebraska U.S. House seat, citing his father’s terminal illness.

Mehdi interviewed the outspoken, Oscar-winning Spanish actor last night, just hours after he returned to his hotel from the World Cup Final in New Jersey. The conversation with Bardem touched on everything from the game itself and the performance of the two teams, to Donald Trump’s post-match antics, to Israel’s “impunity” for its “genocide” in Gaza, as Bardem put it.

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledthree Palestinians in attacks on Gaza in the last 24 hours. The strikes came hours after Israeli politicians, including ultranationalist Ministers Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich,marchedtoward Gaza to call for the re-establishment of three illegal settlements.
  • 🇵🇸 More deadly settler violence:Israeli firereportedlykilled at least two Palestinians during a settler attack in the occupied West Bank village of Deir Jarir yesterday. As villagers attempted to confront the settlers, they were met with gunfire from the settlers and Israeli forces. Israeli soldiers then reportedly fired upon ambulances trying to reach those who had been shot. Meanwhile, the Israeli governmentcleareda legal hurdle to using Nile crocodiles around prisons, a plan pushed by Ben-Gvir.

  • 🇬🇧

    Handy Andy:As he becomes Britain’s seventh prime minister in 10 years today, Andy Burnham’s in-tray will include dealing with atypically bizarre demandfrom Trump: to exploit North Sea oil and get rid of windmills in Aberdeen.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    New attacks:Russian strikeskilledat least six in Kyiv and other cities Sunday, highlighting Ukraine’s need for U.S.-made Patriot air defense systems.
  • 🇸🇩 Massacre alert:Former Al Jazeera editor Barry Malonewarnedof “the potential for a massacre in the Sudanese city of el-Obeid,” which is surrounded by “fighters from the Rapid Support Forces paramilitary group… already pounding it with drone attacks.”

Crocodile-infested moats, fantasized about by Donald Trump and now actually pursued by Israel, are the stuff of legend, not reality. But one castle in Czechia has housed creatures in its moat for more than 300 years. What kind?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!


FIFA chief Gianni Infantino tries to stop Donald Trump photo-bombing Spain’s moment of World Cup glory. Never was an undignified trot more richly earned.

    Energy Secretary Chris Wrighttold ABC’s ‘This Week’ Donald Trump’s war with Iran would come to an end “in the next few weeks. Could be sooner than that.” Four months on, as each side continued to strike the other and as new U.S. deaths were announced, Wrighttold the same show: “We needed four to six weeks for military actions. They went for five-and-a-half weeks. We did a pause to try to get an agreement with the Iranian people to allow oil to flow without them attacking it. They’ve proven unwilling to do that. And we’ve pivoted.”
  • Trump Border Czar Tom Homanconceded that if immigration agents who stopped and killedLorenzo Salgado Araujoin Houston did not activate emergency lights on their vehicles, the 52-year-old Mexican citizen could indeed have feared he was being robbed, explaining his reluctance to stop. item.title: The Arrested Tate Brothers Are Tied to the Trumps: Why Won’t Dems Exploit This?
item.description: ⚽ The World Cup may be over, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders! Good…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
⚽ The World Cup may be over, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders!

Good morning, and Happy Monday again, and congratulations to Spain. Martin here, in Washington, DC, delighted as ever to be bringing you the news you need to know to start your week.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we look at the dramatic arrest in Florida of the Anglo-American brothers Andrew and Tristan Tate, wanted on a raft of serious charges in the UK, and ask why top Democrats don’t seem interested in making clear how close the Tates have grown to the Trump family and the Republican Party they own. We also consider the latest catastrophic re-escalation of Donald Trump’s illegal war with Iran, and consider Troy Jackson’s barnstorming display in Maine, where he stands on the brink of confirmation as the Democratic U.S. Senate nominee.

In Miami on Saturday, U.S. marshals arrested the Anglo-American manosphere influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate. In the UK, the brothers face a total of 59 charges, covering rape, sexual assault, trafficking, assault, and offenses related to indecent images of a child and extreme pornography.

Matt Jury, a lawyer who represents several alleged British victims of Andrew Tate, hailed “the real prospect of extradition… after years of public campaigning by the four British women I represent,” adding: “Andrew and Tristan Tate are accused of some of the most serious offenses. It is time they face justice.”

For the brothers, lawyer Joseph McBride said: “The world knows Andrew and Tristan Tate are innocent. Their enemies know it best of all. That is exactly why they have been attacked. We are confident that once a competent judge sees the facts, and once the Department of Justice confronts this egregious abuse of its own authority, Andrew and Tristan Tate will walk free. America does not do Britain’s political dirty work.”

But plenty of Americans have done political dirty work on behalf of the Tates and it seems incredible, particularly in light of ongoing scandal over sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and his links to Donald Trump, that senior Democrats with national platforms and profiles did not this weekend seize on connections between the Tates, the Trumps, and the Republican Party, in order to flood the zone with comment and questions, and shine a harsh spotlight firmly the way of the White House.

It’s not as if material is lacking.

Consider the dreadful account provided just last month by Heidi Blake’s shattering New Yorker exposé. Noting that Andrew Tate has long been “an enthusiastic supporter of Donald Trump” – with Tate saying, “He’s grabbing bitches by the pussy, I like that guy” – Blake tied the Tates and the Trumps firmly together, not least in examining how President Trump’s special envoy, Ric Grenell, appeared last year to secure their freedom to come to the U.S. from Romania, where they face further serious charges.

In 2016, via Donald Trump Jr., Andrew Tate secured an invitation to Trump Tower, getting a photo with the president-elect and bragging about “access to the president’s son.” McBride, now the Tates’ lawyer, was a friend of Trump Jr. too. Later, Blake noted, Tate made a deal with the right-wing streaming service Rumble, where investors included JD Vance, Donald Trump’s future vice-president, and tech mogul Peter Thiel, Vance’s ideological sponsor. In December 2022, when the Tates were arrested in Romania on sex-trafficking charges, Donald Trump Jr. called the case “absolute insanity” and defended them in public.

MAGA influencers from Tucker Carlson to Charlie Kirk praised the Tates too. In the run-up to the 2024 election, Andrew Tate developed relationships with Trump Jr. and with Barron Trump, the president’s youngest son. After Donald Trump survived an assassination attempt in Pennsylvania, Andrew Tate later told reporters: “I’m very close with the Trump family. I know them well. I spoke to Barron after the incident. I look forward to once I’m free being with Donald Trump in person, and reminding him that he’s a bulletproof badass.”

In the weeks before election day, Blake wrote, Tate “pumped out pro-MAGA content, mobilizing young men in such large numbers that Kamala Harris later named him as a key factor in her defeat. When the result was in, Tate posted jubilantly on X: ‘THE PATRIARCHY IS BACK.’ After the inauguration, he added, ‘The Tates will be free, Trump is the president.’”

Vance followed the Tates on X. A young Tate lawyer, Paul Ingrassia, was picked as White House liaison to the DOJ. Alina Habba, the New Jersey parking garage lawyer who became a close Trump aide, appeared on a podcast with Andrew Tate, telling him she was a “big fan” and comparing his legal travails to those of President Trump (who was found liable in a civil trial for sexual abuse). When the Tates were allowed to travel to the U.S., longtime Trump adviser Roger Stone celebrated online.

In February 2025, the brothers arrived in Florida. According to ProPublica, the White House intervened to return electronic devices seized when the Tates landed. After Andrew Tate left the country, he was accused of sexual assault. When the Epstein files were released, Tate disparaged the victims. In Las Vegas and Miami, the brothers attended UFC fights. CEO Dana White, a close Trump ally, offered personal greetings.

Florida Governor Ron DeSantis has spoken against the Tates and announced a criminal investigation. Regardless, they have cultivated GOP ties. Last week, the brothers were in DC. According to the Daily Mail, they were guests of Paolo Zampolli, another Trump special envoy who says that in his days as a modeling agency boss (and Epstein associate) he introduced Trump to Melania Knauss. Zampolli told the Mail the Tates were in town for

The Mail also published a picture of the brothers on Capitol Hill with MAGA Texas Rep. Wesley Hunt, in his congressional office. The Mail quoted a Hunt source as saying, “Wesley was beyond excited to meet with them.” A spokesman for the Tates said: “Washington knows that Andrew and Tristan Tate’s ability to influence the next election is unmatched. Republicans and Democrats love them. Both parties are inviting them to all kinds of interesting events because both parties understand the same thing: the Tate brothers command the loyalty of millions of young men and a growing number of women. All of them vote.’”

Then, on Saturday, the U.S. Marshals swooped. The Tates are due in federal court in Miami next week. Surely – surely – Democrats must now realize that voters need to know more about the web of ties between these accused rapists and traffickers and the president’s family. To reach for the obvious comparison, imagine if Joe Biden was still in office and his son, Hunter, was revealed to be close to two men accused of sexual assault and trafficking. Would the GOP hold back?

To use the terminology of another Anglo import now leaving U.S. shores – the World Cup – it’s a glaring open goal. Are the Democrats really going to miss this one, too?

  • U.S. deaths:At least three troops werekilled, including one in Iraq during the disposal of an Iranian drone, the Pentagon said, a day after Iranian missile strikes on Jordan killed two. Remains recovered there were believed to be those of a soldier first declared missing. The U.S. death toll stood at 17, the wounded at more than 420.
  • Iranian casualties:The Iranian Health Ministrysaidat least 50 people had been killed and 517 wounded since the war restarted earlier this month.
  • Intense exchanges:U.S. and Iranian strikes continued into Monday, both sides targeting critical infrastructure. Iran said a construction site for a nuclear power plant was hit. The IAEAsaidthe site contained no nuclear material when it was last inspected.
  • Slow Strait:Monitorssaidtraffic in the Strait of Hormuz remained low, despite U.S. Central Commandsayingit was “degrad[ing] Iran’s ability to threaten commercial shipping.” Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard said two vessels were in an “accident.” A British military monitorreporteda ship ablaze off Oman.
  • Lebanon latest:President Joseph AounmetSecretary of State Marco Rubio ahead of a White House meeting on Tuesday. At issue: how to implement an agreement leading to Israeli withdrawals from the south of the country.

Former Maine Senate president Troy Jackson is on the brink of securing the Democratic nomination for U.S. Senate, to replace Graham Platner against Republican incumbent Susan Collins.

Jackson, a logger from the north of the state who was third in the primary for governor, dominated Saturday caucuses staged after Platner, a populist progressive who dominated the Senate primary, pulled out over allegations of sexual assault.

Voting continued Sunday, and the nominee will not be made official until next Saturday, but the choice was all but made after Democrats overwhelmingly voted to send Jackson’s slate of delegates to the party’s convention.

Jackson’s major rivals dropped out. Secretary of State Shenna Bellows saluted “every volunteer who worked around the clock for this movement” and said: “That energy is exactly what we need to beat Susan Collins in November – and Democrats don’t have a day to waste in unifying around that shared goal.” Nirav Shah said Maine Democrats were “united, angry, and energized.”

Collins, seeking a sixth six-year term, is the only Republican running for re-election in a state won by Kamala Harris in 2024.

Jackson, who had Bernie Sanders’s endorsement for governor, appealed to Platner supporters. In Portland on Friday, he said: “A lot of people… believed that Maine could send a real working-class fighter to the United States Senate, and that hope does not belong to one person. That movement does not disappear.” A short statement Saturday echoed Platner’s tone, saying: “I’ve been getting text messages all day about, you know, what a great job I did, and I didn’t do a goddamn thing. All of you just smoked it… We’re well on our way to getting the government we fucking deserve. And that’s what I’m excited about.”

A Jackson supporter, Travis Jones of Seal Harbor, told Politico he “was a big supporter of… Platner. I really liked his policies. Troy mirrors a lot of those. The previous campaign really energized me, and here I am today.”

  • Goodbye World Cup:104 matches across three countries over five weeks. That was the 2026 World Cup, which ended on Sundayin New Jersey at MetLife Stadium, as Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in the final and became champions for the second time. Argentina, down to 10 men after a red card, became the first team in World Cup history to fail to record a single shot on target during regular time in a final. Donald Trump, meanwhile, became the first U.S. president ever to attend a World Cup final, where he wasbooed,appearedto fall asleep, and… well… scroll down to see what he did at the end.Sigh.
  • ICE chills:ICE shared Medicaid data with Palantir, the tech firm run by rightwinger Peter Thiel, filingsshowed. TheNew York Timesreportedthat federal agents have been told the FBI will no longer investigate confrontations with immigration agents,and describeda Chicago U.S. attorney’s office in chaos overfailedimmigration cases. The federal governmentactivatedan “alien terrorist” deportation court, created in 1996 but not used.
  • ‘War criminal’:New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani said he was reviewing whether his administration could arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu during the UN General Assembly in September. “I believe that Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” MamdanitoldtheNew York Times. “He’s a war criminal.” Lest we forget, Mamdani first made his much-discussed pledge to arrest Netanyahu in aninterviewwithZeteoandMehdiin December 2024.
  • Israeli effort:Israel has a $45 million contract with former Trump campaign chief Brad Parscale, for an attempt to arrest a trend against Israel in U.S. public opinion, theWall Street Journalreported. In Michigan, Democratic Rep. Haley Stevensavoidedcomment on her $30 million support by AIPAC as she attempted to win a Senate seat. Progressive candidate Abdul El-Sayed wasattackedby Pennsylvania Democratic (in name only) Senator John Fetterman – in part, for being a Muslim.
  • Place your bets:Florida GOP Rep. Anna Paulina Luna tipped off MAGA influencer Rogan “DC Draino” O’Handley about the impending selection of JD Vance as Trump’s running mate in 2024, allowing him to place a winning bet, theWSJreported. Luna denied saying she had done so.
  • Run ends:Theinsurgentindependent Austin Ahlmanendedhis campaign for a Nebraska U.S. House seat, citing his father’s terminal illness.

Mehdi interviewed the outspoken, Oscar-winning Spanish actor last night, just hours after he returned to his hotel from the World Cup Final in New Jersey. The conversation with Bardem touched on everything from the game itself and the performance of the two teams, to Donald Trump’s post-match antics, to Israel’s “impunity” for its “genocide” in Gaza, as Bardem put it.

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledthree Palestinians in attacks on Gaza in the last 24 hours. The strikes came hours after Israeli politicians, including ultranationalist Ministers Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich,marchedtoward Gaza to call for the re-establishment of three illegal settlements.
  • 🇵🇸 More deadly settler violence:Israeli firereportedlykilled at least two Palestinians during a settler attack in the occupied West Bank village of Deir Jarir yesterday. Israeli soldiers then reportedly fired upon ambulances trying to reach those who had been shot. Meanwhile, the Israeli governmentcleareda legal hurdle to using Nile crocodiles around prisons, a plan pushed by Ben-Gvir.

  • 🇬🇧

item.title: EXCLUSIVE: Javier Bardem Joins Mehdi to Talk Spain's World Cup Win, Palestine, Trump, and More
item.description: The world saw Javier Bardem , legendary Spanish actor, Oscar winner, and one-time Bond villain, sitting in the crowd at MetLife Stadium, supporting Spain at the World Cup Final in New Jersey on Sunday. Just hours after the game wrapped, with Spain defeating Argentina 1-0 to reclaim the World Cup…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The world saw Javier Bardem, legendary Spanish actor, Oscar winner, and one-time Bond villain, sitting in the crowd at MetLife Stadium, supporting Spain at the World Cup Final in New Jersey on Sunday.

Just hours after the game wrapped, with Spain defeating Argentina 1-0 to reclaim the World Cup trophy after 16 years, Bardem headed back to his hotel room and joined Mehdi for an exclusive live chat on YouTube and Substack.

The two of them discussed the game itself and Argentina’s poor performance; Donald Trump embarrassing himself during the trophy presentation; whether the match was a proxy for Palestine vs Israel, given the latter’s loud support for Argentina and Spain’s backing of the Palestinians in Gaza; and also, the Spanish government’s anti-war stance on Iran.

The actor told Mehdi that it was important to be able to speak out against what he called the “genocide” in Gaza and the “war criminal” Benjamin Netanyahu without being accused of, or engaging in, antisemitism. In fact, the conversation touched on everything from the “crazy impunity” enjoyed by Israel to the way in which Donald Trump and Javier Milei don’t represent their people to the ridiculously high ticket price at World Cup games in 2026.

Bardem also revealed his plans for the theater stage and plugged his forthcoming movie with his wife, Penelope Cruz.

It is a conversation, above, that you will not want to miss, and which we are releasing without a paywall.

If you’re already a paid subscriber to Zeteo, thank you so much for your support, but do also consider becoming a donor if you can. If you’re a

item.title: 'Zeteo Is a Pathetic Fake News Blog,' Says an Angry Trump White House
item.description: ⚽️ The World Cup may be ending, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders! At…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
⚽️ The World Cup may be ending, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders!

At the beginning of this month, we pissed off the Trump White House.

Again.

We ran a story on how President Donald Trump is already planning to steal classified U.S. documents after he leaves office a second time – just like he did the first time. The West Wing got huffy about it.

“Zeteo is a pathetic fake news blog and their so-called sources have no idea what they’re talking about. Zeteo’s three readers already know this ‘publication’ is total fake news,” the White House told us in an email. No one signed their name. (If you read their statement closely, you’ll notice they did not deny any of our reporting… 🤣🤣🤣)

The federal government is waging a nonstop war on the First Amendment in ways that we haven’t seen in the United States in a long, long time. On top of that, senior White House officials constantly hurl childish taunts and bullshit smears at news organizations that shine a light on what this corrupt, out-of-his-mind president actually does.

Their enemies list includes Zeteo.

The Trump White House has publicly denounced us as “ terrorist sympathizers,” because we’ve reported on the

Top MAGA ally and the world’s richest man, Elon Musk, has been paying attention to what Mehdi and Zeteo have been doing too and… he doesn’t approve! Trump’s “rapid response” team dubbed Mehdi a terrorist-loving “moron,” simply because he called out the White House press secretary’s bald-faced lies. The White House previously branded Zeteo “

They know what we are. And they’re going to keep knowing.

I’ve been a political reporter at multiple mainstream media outlets, including the Daily Beast and Rolling Stone, and I can honestly say that I’ve never been prouder of the work I’ve done than when it’s here with the hard-charging team at Zeteo. You can tell a lot about someone from their enemies. Clearly, Zeteo is getting under Team Trump’s skin; we wouldn’t be doing our jobs right if we weren’t.

But, as always, the only way we can fight back is together.

So if you’re able to, please consider becoming a paid subscriber to

Every paid subscription, every donation, every financial contribution, big or small, sends a message to Donald Trump’s authoritarian, warmongering administration and his party – that we won’t allow ourselves to be bullied, we won’t be silenced, and we won’t stop telling the truth about fascism, genocide, illegal wars, the Republican Party’s widespread efforts to attack free and fair elections, and other moral and literal atrocities of our time.

Please help us fight on. Thank you for standing with Zeteo.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

item.title: Russiagate Was Real, Trump Admits
item.description: ⚽️ The World Cup may be coming to an end, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
⚽️ The World Cup may be coming to an end, but you can support your favorite independent journalism team – Team Zeteo! – year-round with our brand-new football/soccer jerseys! Visit our online store now to purchase them and all of our other Zeteo-branded merch. Paid subscribers get 20% off their orders!

“Russiagate” was real, according to none other than Donald Trump and his administration.

Trump spent years dismissing allegations that Russia interfered in the 2016 U.S. election to aid his presidential campaign, labeling it a “hoax.” And yet, now his administration has, seemingly unwittingly, admitted that Russia sought to meddle in the 2020 election to boost his failed reelection bid.

“President [Vladimir] Putin and senior Russian officials are overseeing efforts by proxies… to spread claims about former Vice President [Joe] Biden as well as Ukrainian politicians and alleged Ukrainian influence in the 2016 election,” reads a declassified National Intelligence Council document published by Trump’s administration on Thursday.

The White House released documents focused on “Election Integrity,” as Trump delivered a primetime national address alleging foreign election interference. And in the trove were documents that countered the president’s own years-long claims questioning intelligence reports indicating that Russia attempted to interfere in U.S. politics specifically on his behalf.

The Trump administration made much ado about the “revelations” people would learn about on Thursday. Members of the government leaked bits and pieces about the groundbreaking claim the president was set to peddle: that China interfered in U.S. elections.

Trump’s allies whipped up the message publicly, too.

“I was just briefed by the White House on what to expect this evening,” Ohio Senator Bernie Moreno tweeted ominously on Thursday. “I would encourage every American to tune in tonight to the President’s speech. This may be the most important Oval Office address since the Cuban Missile Crisis.”

What actually unfolded was Trump conspiratorially rehashing vulnerabilities that had been known for years in the U.S. intelligence community – and misrepresenting the facts to suggest that the 2020 election outcome, which he lost, was in question. Trump focused primarily on actions by China, rather than other actors like Russia (or Israel, for that matter).

But the documents that the Trump administration itself released show that Joe Biden, and not Donald Trump, was victim of targeted foreign interference during the 2020 election cycle – and Russia was, per the government’s own findings, the main culprit, much more so than China. Indeed, the National Intelligence Council report states outright that “Beijing did not intend to try to affect the election.”

Not so for Russia. The report says that Russia was using “state media, social media, and other online media platforms to denigrate the former Vice President, the Democratic Party, and the U.S. political ‘establishment’ and to promote divisions in the country.”

Per the report, Russia pushed claims about how Joe Biden, as vice president, “engaged in criminal activity in his dealings with Ukraine and individuals tied to Ukrainian energy firm Burisma.” The document states that actors affiliated with the Russian government were “advancing such narratives with US officials and other prominent persons.”

It continues: “These figures are conspiring to intensify their efforts as the election approaches to orchestrate a high-profile corruption scandal implicating former Vice President Biden and the Democratic Party at the peak of the 2020 US presidential campaign. Their aim is to defeat the former Vice President and ensure the President’s victory.”

One has to wonder if Trump even read the documents he thought were so important to reveal to the public.

The document also adds something that may sound familiar: “Russian actors have circulated narratives about voter fraud resulting from mail-in balloting and called some U.S. primary voting processes ‘rigged’ by the Democratic Party.”

It’s a claim that Trump himself has made an uncountable number of times, including Thursday night, when he said that “if you watched California and so many other places, mail-in ballots are inherently corrupt.”

Meanwhile, Trump has repeatedly claimed that the notion of Russia interfering in U.S. elections was a hoax.

In May 2017, after former FBI Director Robert Mueller was appointed as special counsel to oversee the investigation into alleged Russian interference in the 2016 election and potential ties to the Trump campaign, Trump tweeted, “This is the single greatest witch hunt of a politician in American history!”

In April 2019, after Mueller’s report was released – which concluded that Russia interfered in the 2016 election, but did not establish that Russia conspired with the Trump campaign to do so – Trump reiterated that the investigation was “an Illegally Started Hoax that never should have happened.”

Trump repeated the claim throughout the 2024 election, and even afterwards.

“Obama himself manufactured the Russia, Russia, Russia HOAX,” he posted on his social media app, Truth Social, last year. “Crooked Hillary, Sleepy Joe, and numerous others participated in this, THE CRIME OF THE CENTURY!. Irrefutable EVIDENCE. A major threat to our Country!!!”

A few months later, after sharing a phone call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, Trump told reporters: “Don’t forget, we went through the “Russia, Russia, Russia” hoax together.

And he’d call me, I’d call him. I’d say, ‘Can you believe the stuff that they’re making up?’ And it turned out we were right. They made it all up.”

Of course, it wasn’t just Robert Mueller suggesting Russia had attempted to interfere in U.S. elections.

A 2017 Intelligence Community Assessment concluded that Russia meddled in the 2016 U.S. election, and specifically to aid Trump.

A multi-volume report released by the Republican-led Senate Intelligence Committee between 2019 and 2020 found that the Russian government had pursued an aggressive campaign to interfere in the election on Trump’s behalf, and that there were numerous points of contact between Trump officials and Russian operatives.

The acting head of the committee was none other than Marco Rubio, who at the time said they found “irrefutable evidence of Russian meddling.”

Six years later, as Rubio serves at Trump’s beck-and-call, Trump’s own administration has now released documents attesting that Russia sought to aid his 2020 campaign, too.
