item.title: Trump's Big Speech Was 'Gibberish,' 'Drivel,' 'Horseshit'
item.description: Donald Trump took his obsession with the 2020 election to a whole new level during his latest speech from the White House. In addition to repeating false claims that the election was rigged, he doubled down on claims that China played a role in the rigging. To debunk his conspiratorial and…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump took his obsession with the 2020 election to a whole new level during his latest speech from the White House. In addition to repeating false claims that the election was rigged, he doubled down on claims that China played a role in the rigging. To debunk his conspiratorial and self-serving claims, Mehdi went live with Zeteo’s senior political correspondent Swin Suebsaeng and Maryland Congressman Jamie Raskin, a former House impeachment manager.

“I feel like Donald Trump has gone full George W. Bush this year,” says Mehdi, pointing to Trump’s reliance on “raw” unvetted intelligence while presenting his “outlandish, unsubstantiated, conspiratorial, and self-serving” claims about election interference to the country.

During the livestream, Swin shared some soon-to-be-released original reporting from Zeteo indicating that Trump and senior White House staffers were briefed in advance of tonight that there was no “smoking gun that China actually managed to flip votes in Joe Biden’s direction in 2020.” He also called out the “horseshit" that Trump “spewed” about alleged vote-rigging in key swing states like Michigan.

Raskin, who described Trump’s speech as “gibberish,” “drivel,” and “almost self-debunking,” told Mehdi the Trump administration is “trying to set the predicate for different kinds of attacks on the electoral system.”

Mehdi and Swin pressed the Democratic congressman on how his party plans to hold Republicans to account for all this if they gain control of the House next year, while also asking him about:

  • His colleagues who voted against the amendment to cut aid to Israel

  • Whether he will be endorsing Senate primary candidate Abdul El-Sayed

  • His view on Democrats taking money from AIPAC

Mehdi and Swin weren’t the only ones doing the asking; as with every livestream at Zeteo, you, the subscribers, get to take part in the conversation. From questions on how the Chinese government will respond to this ridiculous Trump speech, to whether his voters are “stupid” enough to believe his lies, our speakers answered as many of your questions as they could.

Be sure to watch the full conversation above and keep an eye out for our next live to get in on the conversation.

This video is being shared without a paywall. If you believe in the work we do and want to see more of it, do consider becoming a paid subscriber. And if you’re not ready for the commitment, then a donation would still go a long way.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: ‘My Neighbor Was Murdered by White Supremacists’: Zeteo Reports From Maine on Yet Another ICE Killing
item.description: After Donald Trump’s primetime address tonight, scheduled at 9pm ET, head to zeteo.com and youtube.com/@zeteo to hear Mehdi , Swin , and Rep. Jamie Raskin break down the litany of lies the president will undoubtedly tell about the 2020 election and more. BIDDEFORD, Maine – Thirty-six hours after…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
After Donald Trump’s primetime address tonight, scheduled at 9pm ET, head to zeteo.com and youtube.com/@zeteo to hear Mehdi, Swin, and Rep. Jamie Raskin break down the litany of lies the president will undoubtedly tell about the 2020 election and more.

BIDDEFORD, Maine – Thirty-six hours after Donald Trump’s masked secret police killed Joan Sebastián Guerrero, his blood is still on the ground, stained into the road.

“This is blood,” someone has written in chalk. There’s a memorial on the sidewalk with bouquets of flowers, candles, notes, cardboard signs, and a drawing of Guerrero. There’s a stuffed bear and a toy lobster. A dozen people stand across the street, outside a pawn shop, protesting solemnly, as cars drive by.

“It felt important to come out and stand for community and connection against destruction and disorder; that’s why I’m still standing here,” Gracelyn Kilpatrick, a 36-year-old Biddeford resident, tells Zeteo, as she holds an American flag. “I’ve been here for about three hours now, and I don’t want to leave this space unattended… I know that there are vigils and protests going on in other places. I think it’s really important to be here.”

Since the start of his second presidency, Trump has brought an intensifying level of disorder and destruction throughout the United States, as he’s turned Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) into an unaccountable paramilitary force that wages war on American cities, kidnaps immigrants, and kills people with impunity, sometimes on video. Republicans – including Maine Senator Susan Collins, who’s up for re-election – have responded to mounting public outrage by defending ICE and showering it with cash.

The circumstances under which ICE first engaged Guerrero, a Colombian immigrant, remain murky. ICE has claimed Guerrero, who was authorized to work in the U.S. and had a Social Security number, attempted to flee in his car, and that an agent shot him out of fear “for public safety.” Video of the aftermath shows Guerrero’s vehicle driving in circles. As an agent tears him out of his car, Guerrero’s lifeless body slams into the ground. Rather than try to administer aid, the agents put handcuffs on him. He was 25.

Neighbors watched and heard Guerrero’s wife and daughter cry out as they looked upon his dead body. Speaking about the daughter, one witness told the Portland Press Herald, “She was still in her Bluey pajamas.” (The paper has now

Talking with Zeteo, Kilpatrick says, “For that to happen in front of his family and his 3-year-old daughter, it’s shocking.”

And yet, Guerrero’s senseless death wasn’t entirely a shock. Trump’s administration has had Maine in its crosshairs since at least the winter, when it surged ICE agents to the state as part of “Operation Catch of the Day” – named as such because, for Team Trump, dehumanizing people and tearing families apart aren’t just policy priorities, but something to laugh about.

“I know what it looks like when shit’s bad, and this is bad,” Troy Sargent, a 40-year-old veteran, tells Zeteo at the protest, across the street from where Guerrero’s blood has stained the road. “There’s blood in the street right there… and it doesn’t really matter the reason – whether he was undocumented, it doesn’t matter. You don’t do that.”

Sargent says he’s been a teacher for over a decade. “Most of my students have been either first-generation or their parents came over here, and they don’t have a lot of English proficiency, and they are the best people I’ve ever known,” he says. “They’re the nicest families. They’re so hardworking, and that’s what I think makes America great.”

Speaking of Trump, Sargent says, “Seeing the way that he has demonized and coalesced this group of ignorant, angry people to hate people who are just here trying to make a life for themselves and who would love to be proud Americans, I can’t stand by that… If they don’t think that we’re out here giving a damn, then they’re going to keep doing this.”

Several journalists film stand-ups on the street corners, with the protest in the background, as reporters mill about the neighborhood, noticeable in their dress shirts. Some are “from away,” as Mainers would say. While Guerrero’s slaying has traumatized the local community, it is a national political story, too, and justifiably so: His death is the inevitable outcome of policy decisions made by Trump and Republicans in DC. They do not plan to end the violence. It will continue.

At nearby Mechanics Park, on Main Street, a dozen or so protesters are loudly chanting “ICE out! ICE out!” as cars pass by. Some honk. Along the fence, overlooking the Saco River, there’s another memorial with notes and flowers and signs. There’s a photo of Guerrero with his wife and baby.

“ICE = Nazi Scum,” one sign says. “Biddeford was built by immigrants,” says another. A couple holds up a painting that says “American genocide, coming to your community.”

“I’m here today because my neighbor was murdered by white supremacists,” says Jessica, a 32-year-old protester, who lives on Guerrero’s street. “Immigrants make Biddeford special… Anytime I’ve ever needed help with anything, a car stuck in the snow, if they see you struggling, my immigrant neighbors show up in multiples. They don’t need to be asked. They come to help.”

She says “it was an absolute horror to wake up and look out my window to a crime scene,” adding: “ICE is terrorizing my community and killing innocent people.”

Another protester, Katie, tells Zeteo, “I don’t think that people should leave the streets until justice is had… I’m not going to let this corner be empty.”

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: A Pro-Trump Private Investigator Is Investigating Elections in Georgia
item.description: After Trump’s primetime address tonight, scheduled at 9pm ET, head to zeteo.com and youtube.com/@zeteo to hear Mehdi and Swin break down the litany of lies the president will undoubtedly tell. Georgia may be one of the most important states in the United States for deciding the balance of power in…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
After Trump’s primetime address tonight, scheduled at 9pm ET, head to zeteo.com and youtube.com/@zeteo to hear Mehdi and Swin break down the litany of lies the president will undoubtedly tell.

Georgia may be one of the most important states in the United States for deciding the balance of power in Washington next year. And in the lead-up to November’s midterms, the state’s election board is working overtime to sow as much distrust as possible in the election results.

In support of these efforts, the Republican-led Georgia State Election Board has hired two election deniers to work as taxpayer-funded investigators. Those men, backed by state subpoena power, get to carry out probes based on their belief in President Donald Trump’s election lies.

These efforts and more are the byproduct of Trump’s incessant lies about the 2020 election, which a vast majority of Georgia Republicans falsely believe was stolen or rigged against Trump – despite a complete lack of evidence.

With control of Congress up for grabs and Democrats pledging to act as a check on Trump’s power, the Republican majority on the State Election Board – whom Trump called his “pitbulls fighting for honesty, transparency, and victory” at a 2024 rally in Atlanta – is leading the Georgia election-denial movement’s efforts to convince people that the November elections can’t be trusted, and that Democrats can only win if there is widespread fraud. At the center of those efforts are debunked fraud claims from 2020 in plurality-Black Fulton County, where the state’s largest Democratic voting bloc could decide the balance of power in Congress.

The board’s tactics include: challenging voter registrations in a largely-Democratic county based on flawed lists that have claimed alive Georgians are dead, which has not been previously reported; constant probing of debunked 2020 fraud claims; coordination with Trump administration and Justice Department officials to amplify those claims; attempting to implement rules that would require the complete elimination of the state’s voting machines in favor of handmarked paper ballots; and demanding access to the secretary of state’s vote-counting operation under the false notion that votes have been manipulated there.

The state election board has also now hired Trump-supporting election deniers to conduct specious investigations into things like minor polling location changes in a Republican county and long lines there based on Facebook comments.

In recent months, the Georgia State Election Board has hired two new investigators, Jason Frazier and Larry Duckworth.

Frazier is a well-known election denier who has personally challenged the registration of hundreds of thousands of registered voters, and lost a lawsuit to be seated on the Fulton County election board.

Duckworth is a licensed private investigator whose Facebook page contains references to conspiracy theories about non-citizen voting and expressions of open support for Trump. “I stand with Trump,” reads a post on Duckworth’s page from March 2023.

“We Must Counter These Liberal Dishonesties to Destroy Elections by Citizens Only,” Duckworth wrote in March 2024, alleging that Democrats were encouraging undocumented immigrants to vote.”

In August 2024, Duckworth proclaimed: “The cheat is on!”

Neither Frazier nor Duckworth have any experience administering elections.

Recently, Duckworth forced Cherokee County election officials to respond to claims of long lines at polling locations in the June primaries – claims that stemmed from Facebook comments viewed by the state election board’s executive director, James Mills, who had no experience in election administration prior to being appointed to the board by the state GOP.

Recently, the State Election Board has also undertaken the unprecedented move of directly challenging the voter registration of 220 voters in Chatham County, home to Savannah and another largely Democratic bloc of voters. The board utilized a software program called “ELLY” to identify the voters.

ELLY is the brainchild of Dr. Rick Richards, a hardcore election denier who was previously behind the EagleAI program that sought to use public databases to identify voters who may have been ineligible to vote based on address changes.

But ELLY is flawed, says Anne Dover, who has been the elections director in Cherokee County for the last 19 years. In early June, the State Election Board asked if Dover would test ELLY’s ability to flag ineligible voters. After only a short time using the system, Dover said she found too many false positives to continue.

On June 8, Dover began by searching for voter information for some of her employees, she told Zeteo. In one instance, ELLY had flagged the wife of a Cherokee County elections board member as being dead.

“One of my board members’ wives was on the obituary list and she is very much alive,” Dover said.

Dover then used ELLY to look up her own voting history and that of her husband.

“My spouse’s record – it showed him voting early and on election day in the same election,” Dover said, noting that, if correct, that would have been a crime. Dover said her husband did not, in fact, vote twice in the same election.

ELLY also had incorrect information about her own voting history, according to Dover.

“It showed me voting in person on election day but I voted by mail,” she said. “When I saw as many errors as I saw, I did not go any further with it.”

Dover then told the state election board that ELLY had failed her brief test of the system. Salleigh Grubbs – a board member who became famous in election-denial circles for chasing down trucks she falsely believed contained shredded ballots in 2020 – responded that ELLY’s flaws were the result of “incorrect data from the secretary of state,” according to Dover.

Grubbs and the other members of the State Election Board did not respond to Zeteo’s request for comment.

In late June, the state election board used the ELLY system to challenge the eligibility of 220 voters in Chatham County, Zeteo has found. This mass voter challenge, ELLY’s flaws uncovered in Cherokee County, and Duckworth’s belief in election conspiracies have not been previously reported.

Along with a state senator and election-denial activists, the state election board is also demanding access to what election skeptics in Georgia have deemed Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger’s “secret bunker.”

The “bunker” is where Raffensperger, a Republican, changes votes to help Democrats, the board’s Republicans believe. In reality, it’s simply a room where the secretary of state’s employees receive vote totals from counties and post them online for the public to see on election night.

At its most recent meeting, the newest Republican member of the Georgia State Election Board – appointed to her seat by the state GOP – cast the deciding vote to demand access to Raffensperger’s so-called “bunker.”

Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr, a Republican, told the board in a letter prior to its most recent meeting on July 8 that it have no authority to access the Secretary of State’s election night operations.

The board has also passed emergency rules that would require counties to use handmarked paper ballots in November in certain circumstances – a key goal of the election-denial movement as a result of its distrust of voting machines. The board also continues to push for the use of handmarked paper ballots statewide, which would force counties to choose between complying with those rules or risk running afoul of a law that was recently extended by the state legislature allowing for the use of ballots that employ QR codes to tabulate votes.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, a Republican, called the legislature into a special session to deal with the QR code provision, which will allow Georgia’s 159 counties to use the same ballots and machines in November’s election as have been used in past elections. The state election board continues to try to undo the legislature’s work.

Meanwhile, Frazier and Duckworth are roaming the state as the board’s new investigators.

Recently, Duckworth sat down with Dover and other election officials in Cherokee County to discuss a complaint that polling precincts had been consolidated. There was just one problem: No precincts had been consolidated. Instead, the owners of a building briefly said that it could no longer serve as a polling location.

“He literally did not know the difference between a polling location and a precinct,” Dover told Zeteo.

Duckworth has professed belief in election conspiracies about noncitizen voting and expressed support for Trump on his Facebook page, Zeteo has found. In March 2024, Duckworth shared a Fox story about a 2021 executive order from former President Joe Biden that Republicans have resurfaced under the oversimplified claim that Biden was “attempting to register” undocumented immigrants to vote.

“We Must Counter These Liberal Dishonesties To Destroy Elections By Citizens Only,” Duckworth wrote. “They hate normal America, because it sees through the lies. They need to have government dependent, failed people to support their power for power’s sake all-consuming passion. Dangerous!”

In August 2024, Duckworth wrote that “the cheat is on” and linked to a false story about a migrant advocacy group telling undocumented immigrants to vote for Biden in that year’s election.

When contacted by Zeteo, Duckworth initially said he had “not posted anything on Facebook about election matters.” In a lengthy conversation, Duckworth then vacillated between defending the posts and claiming he couldn’t be sure if the posts actually came from him. Duckworth admitted to supporting Trump in the 2024 election but said he wasn’t “a MAGA guy,” adding that “MAGA and Trump are different.”

Duckworth claimed that his personal political beliefs play no role in his investigations.

“I am a very non-partisan guy doing these investigations,” Duckworth said. “They’re done very factually, very non-partisan, down the middle. I don’t have any bias one way or the other about what the facts are.”

Chloe Hall contributed to this report.

Justin Glawe writes American Doom, a newsletter that focuses on right-wing extremism and other threats to democracy. Have a tip? Contact Justin Glawe on Signal at justinglawe.20.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Lindsey Graham’s Death Reminded Us What’s Wrong With Mainstream Media
item.description: Hello, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we discuss how Lindsey Graham’s passing exposed just how useless the Sunday shows really are; if the…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Hello, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we discuss how Lindsey Graham’s passing exposed just how useless the Sunday shows really are; if the broadcast networks will do Donald Trump’s bidding and air his election denial address; and what the California attorney general thinks about the damage Bari Weiss has done to CBS. Also, we give you another look inside the Trump-Fox Feedback Loop!

I can think of no more fitting tribute to Lindsey Graham, a man who spent his life opportunistically chasing access and proximity to power while shamelessly preening for the television cameras, than the embarrassing display put on by the political mainstream media in covering his death.

This was best typified by the Sunday shows. Namely, CNN’s ‘State of the Union’ and NBC News’s ‘Meet the Press,’ which saw both programs’ anchors get pathetically rolled in order to say they scored an interview with the president.

Graham, who suddenly passed away this weekend from a ruptured aorta, had been a staple of the Sunday morning shows ever since his “Three Amigos” days, when he was the wingman of fellow war hawk John McCain. Upon his passing, for instance, CBS News’s ‘Face the Nation’ noted that Graham had made roughly 100 appearances on the show throughout his Capitol Hill career.

In fact, anchor Kristen Welker pointed out that Graham was scheduled to make his 64th appearance on ‘Meet the Press’ this past Sunday before his unexpected death, presumably to speak about his recent trip to Ukraine, argue for greater Russian sanctions, and push for increased military action against Iran.

The South Carolina lawmaker was so determined to keep his TV hit that he reportedly told someone that, even though he wasn’t feeling well on Saturday, he would wait until after the interview to go see a doctor.

In his place, Welker welcomed both Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu “to reflect on his life and legacy.” That’s right. An American lawmaker suddenly dies, and one of the people you turn to is the leader of another country.

During her conversation with Netanyahu, Welker revealed that she had spoken to Graham multiple times throughout the past week, noting that Graham “thought that a more robust military operation was needed” against Iran now that Trump had declared the ceasefire over.

While Netanyahu engaged with her about Iran, largely through the lens of Graham’s “unabashed” support for all-out war, Welker had no such luck with Trump. Towards the end of her phone interview with the president, she attempted to get his reaction to Iran declaring the Strait of Hormuz closed, only for Trump to quickly shut her down and assert she was violating the call’s ground rules.

“It’s open, and I don’t want to talk about it because I want to honor the life of Lindsey Graham. So, I don’t want to talk about it. I told you that before the call,” he groused, prompting Welker to move on.

It was Trump’s call with ‘State of the Union’ moderator Jake Tapper, however, that drew the most attention – and not in a good way.

After spending a few minutes listening to the president ramble about how Graham was going to make sure Trump’s prized SAVE America Act would pass before the senator’s untimely death, the CNN anchor tried to sneak in a question about Iran – only to get scolded in return.

“Iran has declared the Strait of Hormuz closed. Is that true?” Tapper wondered, prompting Trump to fume: “It’s open as far as we’re concerned. Don’t talk about it. Talk about the reason you asked me to speak.”

After demurring in order to give Trump a “final word” about his deceased ally, which consisted of a meandering response about Graham’s defense of Brett Kavanaugh being a “top 5 moment” in Senate history, Tapper extended an invitation to the president to sit down for another interview in the near future.

Instead, the only thing extended was the humiliation of CNN at the hands of the president – who reinforced the narrative that he’s on board with the pending Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery merger in order to make the network he’s long reviled as “fake news” more MAGA-friendly.

“Sure. We’re trying to have CNN go on a normal path. And we’ll do that,” Trump said, seemingly lumping himself in with Paramount chief David Ellison, who has reportedly told Trump he’d make “sweeping changes” at CNN if the Warner deal goes through.

Tapper’s response? “Well, I’m on a normal path right here.” Trump seemingly approved. “Good. You are,” the president said. The entire exchange prompted several media critics to knock the CNN veteran for his “embarrassing” performance. Worse yet, it allowed others to once again accuse Tapper of “auditioning” for Bari Weiss, the anti-woke CBS News chief who could potentially be in charge of CNN.


And while Tapper would look to save face the following day on ‘The Lead,’ delivering a searing monologue about the president’s “un-American” crackdown on press freedoms, the damage had already been done. Especially since Trump ran to the friendly confines of Fox on Monday morning, where he was more than willing to talk in depth about Iran and the Strait of Hormuz – despite being brought on to ostensibly remember his “good friend” Graham, much like his Sunday show appearances.

In fact, as hilariously documented by Jon Stewart on ‘The Daily Show,’ the hosts of ‘Fox & Friends’ could barely get Trump to talk about Graham at times – as he was too busy rambling about his election fraud conspiracies and getting rid of the filibuster to stay focused on his late pal.

So, what was even the point of having Trump on CNN and NBC News this Sunday, other than to say you booked him? Whatever he had to say could have been delivered in a Truth Social post, and when the hosts even tried to stray slightly from the script, they were embarrassed by the president bluntly saying it hadn’t been among the preconditions of the call.

But, of course, this has always been the problem with the Sunday shows, hasn’t it? That it’s not so much about informing the public, but rather about building and maintaining access and proximity to power. Essentially, give the DC establishment a platform to peddle their talking points while catering to an insular crowd of Beltway pundits, power players and legacy media folks.

And this is why Lindsey Graham was the perfect avatar for the Sunday news shows. He literally appeared hundreds of times on these programs, not because his policy positions were popular with average Americans, but because he knew how to play the game – and pandered to the players.

With the president scheduled to deliver a primetime address tonight, which will reportedly see Trump peddle more conspiracy theories and baseless allegations that the 2020 election was “rigged,” the major broadcast networks find themselves in a predicament.

Will they carry this speech live, thus almost assuredly blasting Trump’s unhinged election lies to millions of Americans, or will they decline to air it while instead following up with reporting on what the president said?

As of this morning, ABC, CBS and NBC all remained tight-lipped about their plans when approached by Zeteo. Some Democrats, in the meantime, have continued to urge the networks to keep it off the air. “I think we have an ethical obligation not to air things that undermine our elections and are not rooted in fact,” Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez said on Tuesday.

As media critic Parker Molloy pointed out this week, the “networks keep falling for this,” having broadcast multiple fact-averse Oval Office addresses over the years. She also asserted that the “standard for turning down a president” only seems to apply to Democrats, referencing the major networks passing on primetime speeches from Barack Obama and Joe Biden for being “overtly political.”

Zeteo, of course, will not be airing the speech, scheduled for 9pm ET. But Mehdi and Swin will react to it and fact-check it right after on zeteo.com and youtube.com/@zeteo. Tune in!

Following a dozen states’ antitrust lawsuit challenging Paramount’s merger this week, Rob Bonta – the California attorney general spearheading the litigation – sounded the alarm about Bari Weiss’s right-wing transformation of CBS News.

“As an American – and a proud American who cares deeply about our democracy and the future of it, its durability, its sustainability – I’m very concerned about the reports about what’s happening at CBS,” Bonta told Status’s Oliver Darcy. “And I’m also concerned about the possibility that what’s happening at CBS could be part of an attempt to curry favor with the president who might want something similar to happen at CNN.”

In the meantime, famed lawyer Norm Eisen said that “the deal is in trouble” due to the antitrust issues at play, while other experts noted that the Ellisons may eventually agree to split off CNN in order to keep the deal since the network “is almost immaterial when it comes to other WBD assets.”

Though, as THR’s Alex Weprin wrote, CNN matters greatly to Trump – and Paramount could really use the network’s cash flow to help pay down its huge debt load.

In yet another episode in the endless series of The Trump-Fox Feedback Loop, the right-wing network has gone all in on portraying the Democratic Party as nothing more than “godless” communists – just as Trump has made that a signature piece of the GOP platform.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘ Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 🛑 Trump Can’t Quit His Failing Campaign of Terror and Murder
item.description: On this day in 2002, President George W. Bush announced the formation of what would become the Department of Homeland Security. Concerns about its role in expanding presidential power at the expense of civil liberties have never died down. Welcome to Thursday. It’s Swin , again. We are enduring yet…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2002, President George W. Bush announced the formation of what would become the Department of Homeland Security. Concerns about its role in expanding presidential power at the expense of civil liberties have never died down.

Welcome to Thursday. It’s Swin, again. We are enduring yet another week of watching Donald Trump fail at surrendering to Iran in a war he started and promptly lost. Stateside, the president is losing a very different kind of war, even as he ups the body count on our streets.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we’ll get into just how badly Trump is eating shit in his war on his own people. Plus, in an astonishing development, a rush of Democrats including Nancy Pelosi (!) voted to cut military aid to Israel, while three Trump nominees were made to sweat through hearings on Capitol Hill.

And there’s more: After Trump’s primetime address tonight, set for 9pm ET, be sure to tune into zeteo.com and youtube.com/@zeteo to hear Mehdi and me break down the litany of lies the president will undoubtedly tell.

On Tuesday evening, Zeteo politics editor Andrew Perez messaged me seven words: “His blood is still on the ground.”

Andrew lives in Maine, so he went to Biddeford, where ICE killed a young Colombian man. (We will have more for you on this story soon; watch your inbox.)

His name was Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero. Before him, there was Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, in Houston, Texas. And now we have a third fatality in just over a week: a 28-year-old man in St. Augustine, Florida, who was apparently killed by a tractor-trailer as he ran from ICE agents.

This is only counting one week of bodies – the ones we know about. And it’s the result of President Trump, his White House policy architect Stephen Miller, and the rest of the administration dedicating the past several weeks to dialing their mass-deportation machine back up to their preferred volume of sadism. After the fierce backlash to the Trump-Vance administration’s siege of Minnesota, the Republican Party hatched a plan to curb at least some anti-immigrant brutality until “ after the midterms.” But they couldn’t even muster that. They just couldn’t help themselves.

On Tuesday, the Department of Homeland Security announced a supposed “pause” of the kinds of ICE traffic stops that got Guerrero and Araujo killed for – it appears – no good reason at all. Administration officials kept whispering to Zeteo that we shouldn’t read too much into the pause – it was always meant as a very temporary period of review of (so-called) best practices in the wake of the deaths. The administration couldn’t even keep up that charade for a single day.

By Wednesday morning, President Trump had ordered DHS to reverse course on its pause on traffic stops – proving that he wasn’t going to allow a couple dead bodies to guilt him into even a sliver of due diligence.

Of course he wasn’t. Since last year, Trump and his party have been committing one of the more unspeakably evil acts I’ve ever seen perpetrated on U.S. soil. He simply cannot stop killing people. And he’s doing it as part of an absurdly lawless, hyper-militarized campaign of aggression, fronted by his armed and masked secret police, against the American people. It’s a war not just on migrants, or foreign nationals, or anyone the Trump White House wishes to nonsensically smear as a “terrorist.” It’s Team Trump’s war on the American people and their neighbors – plain and simple. And were the Democratic Party a capable opposition party, it’d be working much harder to stand in Trump’s way.

As bleak as this all truly is, make no mistake: Trump is losing this war, like the perennial loser he is.

For this president, killing is often the only metric by which to measure victory. You see that in Iran, where Trump is constantly humiliated and beaten in spite of all the pointless death he’s inflicted. And you see that in his own country, where the more the bodies pile up, the harder he insists on losing more.

You saw that in Minnesota early this year, where his attempt to turn the place into a killing field only further cratered his poll numbers – including on immigration, once a winning issue. As of this week, Trump’s poll numbers remain near his record, abysmal lows. Even this White House, run by the planet’s most annoying neo-Confederate knuckle-draggers, knows how unpopular their clampdowns and killing sprees are, to the point that they’re actively trying to hide how they conduct them.

MS NOW host Chris Hayes summed it up perfectly this week: “Donald Trump and Stephen Miller lost the country. They lost the country. Everybody [can] see their deportation pogroms were a debacle.”

The blood is on the ground. The pain and suffering is widespread. Because of this, it’s easy to feel like Trump is winning his war on America.

He’s not. The polling data, and the sustained mass protest movement against him, underscore just how much he’s operating from a position not of strength but of violent bullying.

To pampered losers like Trump and Miller, their inability to see the difference is why so many people will needlessly suffer between now and their eventual fall from power.

  • More strikes:The U.S. and Iranexchangedfire for a fifth day, the U.S. targeting military facilities near the Strait of Hormuz in daytime strikes before switchingnorth towards Tehranafter dark and into Thursday. Iran targeted Arab U.S. allies. Iran said seven were killed when multiple missiles hit an army barracks in Barnbur, with more than 300 people wounded across the country. Iranian officials also said a cancer hospital wasevacuateddue to heavy bombing nearby.
  • ‘Want to settle’:At the U.S. Army War College in Pennsylvania, Trump claimed the U.S. was “doing very well,”andsaid the Iranians “want to settle so badly.”
  • ‘Existential war’:Leading Iranians did not sound so keen. Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibafsaid: “We are in an essential and existential war with America.” Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeli Baghaei said: “We currently have no agenda for negotiations, and our focus is entirely on defense.”
  • Dire Strait:As the U.S. reimposed its naval blockade, using Hellfire missiles to disable a Curacao-flagged tanker, the Iranian Revolutionary Guardsaid: “The export of oil and gas from the region will be either for everyone or for no one.” Oil pricesrose.
  • Release secured:Trumptrumpetedthe freeing of an unnamed American woman held in Iran since 2024, which he called “a gesture of goodwill.” Her lawyer lateridentifiedher as Dena Karari.
  • Dead center:Media outletspublishedimages of a huge billboard in downtown Tehran, showing Trump lying in a coffin and slogans including “We kill Trump.”
  • Lebanon latest:After talks in Rome, U.S. officialssaidIsrael and Lebanon were implementing “pilot zone” withdrawals of Israeli troops in the south of the country. Details were few. Trumptold Foxhe would rather focus on “the big leagues,” meaning Iran. Hours later, the Israeli defense ministersaidIsrael is determined “to remain in the security zones in Syria, Gaza and Lebanon.”

Zeteo correspondent Prem Thakker was on Capitol Hill as the House voted on cutting aid to Israel. Here’s his report:

The politics of Israel and Palestine continue to be upended: Yesterday, half the Democratic caucus voted to cut U.S. military aid to Israel.

The House was voting on an amendment led by Republican Rep. Thomas Massie to the annual bill funding the State Department and related agencies. The language would have cut the $3.3 billion in military aid that the U.S. sends to Israel each year.

In the lead-up to the vote, advocates were unclear exactly how many supporters there would be: 40, 60, 80. The most ambitious of guesses – over 100 – was what happened. There were 104 “yes” votes: 103 Democrats, and one Republican, Massie, voted to cut military aid to Israel.

Not long ago, that would have been a completely unthinkable outcome. It followed a historic vote in the Senate, in which almost every Democrat voted to block at least some weapons to Israel.

No less remarkable were some names to be found in those voting for Massie’s amendment:

  • Former Speaker of the House Nancy Pelosi.

  • Rep. Katherine Clark, the current number 2 in party leadership.

  • Pat Ryan, who represents a competitive New York district, and who after casting his vote said he no longer wants AIPAC’s support and would return its funds. Just three years ago, Ryan was among 22 Democrats who voted to censure Rashida Tlaib!

And yes, some may argue the vote was for some cynical, even existential, move to stave off primary challengers. But that such a fear is real is unprecedented too.

Some Democrats in competitive races still voted with Republicans (against half of their colleagues, and a majority of the Democratic and independent voter base) to keep the weapons flowing.

There was Haley Stevens, who is running against Abdul El-Sayed in the Michigan Senate primary. There was Greg Stanton, an Arizona incumbent facing a primary challenge from Kai Newkirk, a democratic socialist.

In any case, the vote was historic, underscoring just how toxic pro-Israel politics is becoming. For your reference, here are the Democrats who voted against the amendment, to keep billions flowing to Israel.

  • Admit one:JD VancetoldJoe Rogan the Trump administration “mishandled the Epstein [files] release.” The VP blamed former AG Pam Bondi, who has blamed acting Attorney General Todd Blanche. Vance also embraced what might once have been dismissed as a conspiracy theory,sayingJeffrey Epstein was “Mossad or CIA or some other deep state… in America or Israel.”
  • Indiscrete service:Agents protecting Vance and his family are growing disillusioned by entitled requests such as taking the VP’s son to a golf lesson in a military helicopter, MS NOWreported. One source said: “They change everything. They don’t stick to their schedules, and that costs shit-tons of taxpayer money.”
  • ‘Intends to kill Muslims’:A Muslim man wasstabbedmultiple times at a Utah mall because of his religion, police said. The victim, identified as Syed Sohail Uddin on a GoFundMe page set up to help with medical expenses, survived, but is “facing multiple surgeries for his hands, heart, and lungs.” Police said the suspect told them that he “intends to kill Muslims.”
  • White House plumbers:Chief of staff Susie Wiles and FBI Director Kash Patel have seized phones in their investigation of leaks to theNew York Timesabout how the new Qatari-gifted Air Force One lacks key security features, CNNsaid.
  • Follow the money:TrumpslammedNew York’smoratoriumon AI data centers, which he called “money machines.” He should know: He and his family are big investors in the industry. In other absurd money news, the Treasuryreleaseda $1 coin with Trump’s face on it.
  • Mr. T:Fresh fromblockingmore promotions for women, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced that the Pentagon will screen testosterone levels in servicemen over 30, and offer boosters.Really.
  • Booked:Joe Biden will release a memoir in November. The 83-year-oldsaidPromise Me, Americawould tell “the complete story” of his presidency.
  • See you in court:Advocacy group DAWNsuedthe Trump administration over sanctions against the International Criminal Court that are used to bar pro-Palestinian human rights advocacy in the U.S.

It was a busy day on Capitol Hill, writes Martin Pengelly, who watched acting Attorney General Todd Blanche commit a gaffe for the ages.

Fielding a softball from Louisiana Republican John Kennedy – “Are you and President Trump friends?” – Blanche said: “I’m his lawyer.”

Attorney generals are supposed to be, y’know… independent. Blanche corrected himself: “Was his lawyer… I met him as his criminal defense attorney.” So he did, for work which, when combined with Blanche’s ruthlessly corrupt lawfare in office, made for a rocky confirmation hearing. Expect Blanche’s answer to Kennedy to appear alongside a previous friendly comment – “I love working for President Trump… ‘Thank you very much. I love you, sir’” – in your socials soon.

Reports of Blanche’s ordeal zoomed in on John Cornyn, the Texan beaten by a Trump-backed challenger who seemed a possible no vote, thanks to Blanche and Trump’s insanely corrupt IRS suit and its thwarted settlement in the shape of a slush fund for criminal Trump supporters and a sweetheart tax deal for the Trump family. Just one GOP no will leave Blanche stuck in committee.

Elsewhere, Director of National Intelligence nominee Jay Clayton was battered by Georgia Democrat Jon Ossoff, whose election in 2020 is set to be part of Trump’s primetime conspiracy circus tonight. See below for a very relevant clip. Erica Schwartz, nominated to lead the CDC, also had a tricky time, over questions about RFK Jr. and vaccines.

Donald Trump has his face on a $1 coin. Between 1928 and 1969, Grover Cleveland, the only other president to serve non-consecutive terms, had a bill. What denomination was it?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel is increasinglyissuingforced displacement orders to Palestinians in central Gaza, Al Jazeera reported, as strikes continue, despite a so-called “ceasefire.” More than 1,120 Palestinians have been killed by Israel since the “ceasefire” last October, more than 73,200 since October 2023.

  • 🇨🇺

    Attack plans:Trump officials have considered options for U.S. military action against Cuba, including assault by the 101st Airborne Division, CBS Newsreported, adding that military commitments in Iran will delay any possible action.
  • 🇬🇧 Nigel farrago:In 2024, Nigel Farage said he would need £1 million (over $1.3 million) a year to come back into UK politics and lead the far-right Reform party,The Guardianreported. A month later, a Thailand-based crypto billionaire gave Farage £5 million (over $6.7 million), a gift now stoking a growing scandal and threatening Farage’s career.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:More health workers went on strike in DR Congo as the fastest-growing outbreak on record continued, casesreaching2,011 with 754 deaths.
  • 🇸🇩 Sudan shocker:Companies owned by a U.S. government and military contractor have operated planes linking regional supply routes to the stronghold of the RSF paramilitary force accused of genocide, Reutersreported.


There’s a reason Republicans go after Jon Ossoff. He’s very good at going after them.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 🛑 Trump Can’t Quit His Failing Campaign of Terror and Murder

Concerns about its role in expanding presidential power at the expense of civil liberties have never died down. Welcome to Thursday. It’s Swin , again. We are enduring yet…

The administration couldn’t even keep up that charade for a single day.

By Wednesday morning, President Trump had ordered DHS to reverse course on its pause on traffic stops – proving that he wasn’t going to allow a couple dead bodies to guilt him into even a sliver of due diligence.

Of course he wasn’t. Since last year, Trump and his party have been committing one of the more unspeakably evil acts I’ve ever seen perpetrated on U.S. soil. He simply cannot stop killing people. And he’s doing it as part of an absurdly lawless, hyper-militarized campaign of aggression, fronted by his armed and masked secret police, against the American people. It’s a war not just on migrants, or foreign nationals, or anyone the Trump White House wishes to nonsensically smear as a “terrorist.” It’s Team Trump’s war on the American people and their neighbors – plain and simple. And were the Democratic Party a capable opposition party, it’d be working much harder to stand in Trump’s way.

As bleak as this all truly is, make no mistake: Trump is losing this war, like the perennial loser he is.

For this president, killing is often the only metric by which to measure victory. You see that in Iran, where Trump is constantly humiliated and beaten in spite of all the pointless death he’s inflicted. And you see that in his own country, where the more the bodies pile up, the harder he insists on losing more.

You saw that in Minnesota early this year, where his attempt to turn the place into a killing field only further cratered his poll numbers – including on immigration, once a winning issue. As of this week, Trump’s poll numbers remain near his record, abysmal lows. Even this White House, run by the planet’s most annoying neo-Confederate knuckle-draggers, knows how unpopular their clampdowns and killing sprees are, to the point that they’re actively trying to hide how they conduct them.

MS NOW host Chris Hayes summed it up perfectly this week: “Donald Trump and Stephen Miller lost the country. They lost the country. Everybody [can] see their deportation pogroms were a debacle.”

The blood is on the ground. The pain and suffering is widespread. Because of this, it’s easy to feel like Trump is winning his war on America.

He’s not. The polling data, and the sustained mass protest movement against him, underscore just how much he’s operating from a position not of strength but of violent bullying.

To pampered losers like Trump and Miller, their inability to see the difference is why so many people will needlessly suffer between now and their eventual fall from power.

  • More strikes:The U.S. and Iranexchangedfire for a fifth day, the U.S. targeting military facilities near the Strait of Hormuz in daytime strikes before switchingnorth towards Tehranafter dark and into Thursday. Iran targeted Arab U.S. allies. Iran said seven were killed when multiple missiles hit an army barracks in Barnbur, with more than 300 people wounded across the country. Iranian officials also said a cancer hospital wasevacuateddue to heavy bombing nearby.
  • ‘Want to settle’:At the U.S. Army War College in Pennsylvania, Trump claimed the U.S. was “doing very well,”andsaid the Iranians “want to settle so badly.”
  • ‘Existential war’:Leading Iranians did not sound so keen. Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibafsaid: “We are in an essential and existential war with America.” Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeli Baghaei said: “We currently have no agenda for negotiations, and our focus is entirely on defense.”
  • Dire Strait:As the U.S. reimposed its naval blockade, using Hellfire missiles to disable a Curacao-flagged tanker, the Iranian Revolutionary Guardsaid: “The export of oil and gas from the region will be either for everyone or for no one.” Oil pricesrose.
  • Release secured:Trumptrumpetedthe freeing of an unnamed American woman held in Iran since 2024, which he called “a gesture of goodwill.” Her lawyer lateridentifiedher as Dena Karari.
  • Dead center:Media outletspublishedimages of a huge billboard in downtown Tehran, showing Trump lying in a coffin and slogans including “We kill Trump.”
  • Lebanon latest:After talks in Rome, U.S. officialssaidIsrael and Lebanon were implementing “pilot zone” withdrawals of Israeli troops in the south of the country. Details were few. Trumptold Foxhe would rather focus on “the big leagues,” meaning Iran. Hours later, the Israeli defense ministersaidIsrael is determined “to remain in the security zones in Syria, Gaza and Lebanon.”

Zeteo correspondent Prem Thakker was on Capitol Hill as the House voted on cutting aid to Israel. Here’s his report:

The politics of Israel and Palestine continue to be upended: Yesterday, half the Democratic caucus voted to cut U.S. military aid to Israel.

The House was voting on an amendment led by Republican Rep. Thomas Massie to the annual bill funding the State Department and related agencies. The language would have cut the $3.3 billion in military aid that the U.S. sends to Israel each year.

In the lead-up to the vote, advocates were unclear exactly how many supporters there would be: 40, 60, 80. The most ambitious of guesses – over 100 – was what happened. There were 104 “yes” votes: 103 Democrats, and one Republican, Massie, voted to cut military aid to Israel.

Not long ago, that would have been a completely unthinkable outcome. It followed a historic vote in the Senate, in which almost every Democrat voted to block at least some weapons to Israel.

No less remarkable were some names to be found in those voting for Massie’s amendment:

  • Former Speaker of the House Nancy Pelosi.

  • Rep. Katherine Clark, the current number 2 in party leadership.

  • Pat Ryan, who represents a competitive New York district, and who after casting his vote said he no longer wants AIPAC’s support and would return its funds. Just three years ago, Ryan was among 22 Democrats who voted to censure Rashida Tlaib!

And yes, some may argue the vote was for some cynical, even existential, move to stave off primary challengers. But that such a fear is real is unprecedented too.

Some Democrats in competitive races still voted with Republicans (against half of their colleagues, and a majority of the Democratic and independent voter base) to keep the weapons flowing.

There was Haley Stevens, who is running against Abdul El-Sayed in the Michigan Senate primary. There was Greg Stanton, an Arizona incumbent facing a primary challenge from Kai Newkirk, a democratic socialist.

In any case, the vote was historic, underscoring just how toxic pro-Israel politics is becoming. For your reference, here are the Democrats who voted against the amendment, to keep billions flowing to Israel.

  • Admit one:JD VancetoldJoe Rogan the Trump administration “mishandled the Epstein [files] release.” The VP blamed former AG Pam Bondi, who has blamed acting Attorney General Todd Blanche. Vance also embraced what might once have been dismissed as a conspiracy theory,sayingJeffrey Epstein was “Mossad or CIA or some other deep state… in America or Israel.”
  • Indiscrete service:Agents protecting Vance and his family are growing disillusioned by entitled requests such as taking the VP’s son to a golf lesson in a military helicopter, MS NOWreported. One source said: “They change everything. They don’t stick to their schedules, and that costs shit-tons of taxpayer money.”
  • ‘Intends to kill Muslims’:A Muslim man wasstabbedmultiple times at a Utah mall because of his religion, police said. The victim, identified as Syed Sohail Uddin on a GoFundMe page set up to help with medical expenses, survived, but is “facing multiple surgeries for his hands, heart, and lungs.” Police said the suspect told them that he “intends to kill Muslims.”
  • White House plumbers:Chief of staff Susie Wiles and FBI Director Kash Patel have seized phones in their investigation of leaks to theNew York Timesabout how the new Qatari-gifted Air Force One lacks key security features, CNNsaid.
  • Follow the money:TrumpslammedNew York’smoratoriumon AI data centers, which he called “money machines.” He should know: He and his family are big investors in the industry. In other absurd money news, the Treasuryreleaseda $1 coin with Trump’s face on it.
  • Mr. T:Fresh fromblockingmore promotions for women, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced that the Pentagon will screen testosterone levels in servicemen over 30, and offer boosters.Really.
  • Booked:Joe Biden will release a memoir in November. The 83-year-oldsaidPromise Me, Americawould tell “the complete story” of his presidency.
  • See you in court:Advocacy group DAWNsuedthe Trump administration over sanctions against the International Criminal Court that are used to bar pro-Palestinian human rights advocacy in the U.S.

It was a busy day on Capitol Hill, writes Martin Pengelly, who watched acting Attorney General Todd Blanche commit a gaffe for the ages.

Fielding a softball from Louisiana Republican John Kennedy – “Are you and President Trump friends?” – Blanche said: “I’m his lawyer.”

Attorney generals are supposed to be, y’know… independent. Blanche corrected himself: “Was his lawyer… I met him as his criminal defense attorney.” So he did, for work which, when combined with Blanche’s ruthlessly corrupt lawfare in office, made for a rocky confirmation hearing. Expect Blanche’s answer to Kennedy to appear alongside a previous friendly comment – “I love working for President Trump… ‘Thank you very much. I love you, sir’” – in your socials soon.

Reports of Blanche’s ordeal zoomed in on John Cornyn, the Texan beaten by a Trump-backed challenger who seemed a possible no vote, thanks to Blanche and Trump’s insanely corrupt IRS suit and its thwarted settlement in the shape of a slush fund for criminal Trump supporters and a sweetheart tax deal for the Trump family. Just one GOP no will leave Blanche stuck in committee.

Elsewhere, Director of National Intelligence nominee Jay Clayton was battered by Georgia Democrat Jon Ossoff, whose election in 2020 is set to be part of Trump’s primetime conspiracy circus tonight. See below for a very relevant clip. Erica Schwartz, nominated to lead the CDC, also had a tricky time, over questions about RFK Jr. and vaccines.

Donald Trump has his face on a $1 coin. Between 1928 and 1969, Grover Cleveland, the only other president to serve non-consecutive terms, had a bill. What denomination was it?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel is increasinglyissuingforced displacement orders to Palestinians in central Gaza, Al Jazeera reported, as strikes continue, despite a so-called “ceasefire.” More than 1,120 Palestinians have been killed by Israel since the “ceasefire” last October, more than 73,200 since October 2023.

  • 🇨🇺

    Attack plans:Trump officials have considered options for U.S. military action against Cuba, including assault by the 101st Airborne Division, CBS Newsreported, adding that military commitments in Iran will delay any possible action.
  • 🇬🇧 Nigel farrago:In 2024, Nigel Farage said he would need £1 million (over $1.3 million) a year to come back into UK politics and lead the far-right Reform party,The Guardianreported. A month later, a Thailand-based crypto billionaire gave Farage £5 million (over $6.7 million), a gift now stoking a growing scandal and threatening Farage’s career.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:More health workers went on strike in DR Congo as the fastest-growing outbreak on record continued, casesreaching2,011 with 754 deaths.
  • 🇸🇩 Sudan shocker:Companies owned by a U.S. government and military contractor have operated planes linking regional supply routes to the stronghold of the RSF paramilitary force accused of genocide, Reutersreported.


There’s a reason Republicans go after Jon Ossoff. He’s very good at going after them.

item.title: ICE Keeps Killing People. Can They Be Held to Account? Legal Expert Weighs In
item.description: It happens again and again: ICE kills someone, and then they’re caught in the same “cover-your-ass lie” – that the killing was self-defense . This week, an immigration officer shot and killed a man in Maine on Monday. Last week, an officer shot and killed a man in Texas. It brings back into the…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
It happens again and again: ICE kills someone, and then they’re caught in the same “cover-your-ass lie” – that the killing was self-defense.

This week, an immigration officer shot and killed a man in Maine on Monday. Last week, an officer shot and killed a man in Texas.

It brings back into the headlines a key question: how do DHS and ICE seem to be able to kill civilians with impunity? Law school professor and top civil rights activist Maya Wiley joins John Harwood to break it all down. This was a Substack Live conversation where Zeteo subscribers could tune in live and ask questions.

Watch the full video above to hear the two discuss how Congress should respond to the killings, whether the Civil Rights movement is still winning 18 months into Trump’s presidency, and Zohran Mamdani vs. Jonathan Greenblatt.

Paid subscribers can watch the full, 37-minute conversation. Free subscribers can watch a 2-minute preview. If you support the work that we do, consider upgrading your subscription to never hit a paywall again.

item.title: ‘Explosive Diarrhea’ Outbreak: What You Need to Know
item.description: An outbreak of cyclosporiasis, which causes “explosive diarrhea,” has surged across the U.S., spreading faster than the federal government can track. The outbreak has left thousands of people in more than half of the states ill, with cases continuing to rise. The source of the infection remains a…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
An outbreak of cyclosporiasis, which causes “explosive diarrhea,” has surged across the U.S., spreading faster than the federal government can track.

The outbreak has left thousands of people in more than half of the states ill, with cases continuing to rise.

The source of the infection remains a mystery.

Where did the parasite come from? Is it contagious? And what role did the Trump administration’s decision to cut federal funding from health agencies play in difficulties tracking the outbreak?

Here are six things you need to know about the cyclosporiasis outbreak:

1. Where did it come from?

The infection is caused by a microscopic parasite. The source for this outbreak has not been identified. The parasite is uniquely difficult to track, as it typically takes one to two weeks for people to start experiencing symptoms.

Perhaps Steven Mandernach, executive director of the Association of Food and Drug Officials, put it best, telling the BBC: “This isn’t like detecting a needle in a haystack. It’s like detecting a microscopic portion of a needle in a haystack.”

2. How is it spreading?

Cyclosporiasis is transmitted by consuming food or water contaminated with feces. Foods associated with cyclosporiasis outbreaks have included raspberries, basil, salad mixes, cilantro, berry mixes, lettuce, and snap peas.

It is unlikely that the illness spreads through person-to-person contact.

3. Why are people blaming the Trump administration?

Under the Trump administration, the CDC removed cyclospora – and several other parasites – from its mandatory reporting list. This was a part of Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s broader effort to cut back on the CDC’s tracking system for food-borne diseases.

In addition, many of those laid off from the CDC by the Trump administration have been employees who used to track these types of outbreaks. Health experts have warned that these changes are making the illness more difficult for the CDC to track, as the number of cases is likely higher than reported.

4. What are the symptoms of cyclosporiasis?

According to the CDC, common symptoms of cyclosporiasis include watery diarrhea, loss of appetite, weight loss, nausea, cramping, and bloating.

There are also less common symptoms, such as vomiting, low-grade fever, headaches, body aches, and other flu-like symptoms.

Typically, those who have the infection will begin to experience symptoms about one week after eating food or drinking water contaminated by the parasite, but symptoms can appear as soon as two days after the exposure or as late as two weeks or more.

Some people who are infected may be asymptomatic, especially where there are outbreaks.

Symptoms can last from a few days to a month or longer without treatment, but doctors can prescribe antibiotics to help treat the infection.

5. Which states are affected by the outbreak?

As of Tuesday afternoon, according to an official health alert, CDC had identified 1,645 cases of cyclosporiasis across the country, with another 5,100 reported by states since May 1. In the same time period in 2025, the CDC reported just 249 cases.

At least 34 states are reporting cases. Roughly half are in Michigan, which has confirmed more than 3,300 infections. New York’s health department has identified just over 400 cases. The CDC suspects that at least 400 cases from across Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and West Virginia are connected.

Since cases were first reported in May, 141 people have been hospitalized with the infection, but no deaths have been reported.

6. How can I avoid cyclosporiasis?

While the source of the outbreak hasn’t been confirmed, Joellen Russell, a climate and integrative science professor at the University of Arizona, told USA Today the one thing people should be most cautious of are bag salad mixes, which are often from more than one grower.

All fruits and vegetables, including pre-washed produce, should be washed before eating, experts say. Michigan’s state health department says cooking produce can also help prevent people from contracting the infection, as the parasite dies at a temperature of 158 degrees Fahrenheit.
